best_practices: Conduct regular risk assessments to align with APRA requirements and the ASD Essential Eight.
Engage with stakeholders throughout the process to ensure comprehensive understanding and support.
Document every stage of the engagement acceptance process thoroughly for transparent record-keeping.
Regularly train staff on regulatory requirements and compliance protocols to maintain a culture of compliance.
Control.description: Documented control objectives suitable for assurance engagement; management's assertion prepared in accordance with criteria.
Control.title: Engagement Acceptance and Continuance (ASAE 3150)
Control.evidence_required: Engagement letter, control objectives schedule, management assertion
Control.implementation_overview:
To effectively implement control CTL-056 under ASAE 3150 for Engagement Acceptance and Continuance, organizations in Australia must ensure they establish documented control objectives and management assertions that align with defined criteria. This involves a collaborative process that involves both automated tools and manual procedures, ensuring that engagement objectives reflect the broader regulatory landscape including the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) and APRA standards. Furthermore, leveraging automated tools can streamline documentation and tracking while manual review processes will ensure compliance and thorough understanding of the risks involved in maintaining assurance engagements.

The implementation must also consider critical frameworks such as the SOCI Act and the ASD Essential Eight, focusing on protecting sensitive information and managing cybersecurity risks. By combining these elements, organizations can create a robust process that not only meets compliance requirements but also builds trust with stakeholders and clients.
common_pitfalls: Failing to adequately involve stakeholders in the engagement process, leading to misaligned objectives.
Neglecting to address evolving regulatory changes, resulting in outdated compliance practices.
Over-reliance on automated solutions without proper manual checks, which can lead to vulnerabilities.
Lack of comprehensive documentation that can create compliance issues during audits.
Control.manual_procedures: Manual procedures include conducting stakeholder interviews to gather insights on risk and control objectives. Additionally, create spreadsheets or documents for tracking engagement assertions manually while ensuring these align with the latest Australian standards and privacy principles. Regularly check and update policies in response to Audit recommendations or regulatory changes.
Control.automation_details: Utilize platforms such as GRC tools (Governance, Risk Management, Compliance) like Archer or MetricStream to automate the tracking and reporting of compliance tasks. Additionally, implementing workflow automation software like Zapier or Microsoft Power Automate can help streamline documentation approvals and stakeholder engagement processes.
Control.testing_and_validation: To test and validate the control, conduct periodic audits to review the documentation of control objectives, verify management assertions, and assess the alignment with the expected criteria. Furthermore, use employee feedback and compliance testing in real scenarios to assess operational effectiveness.

Control.description: System description fairly presents the controls implemented; description aligns with assurance practitioner expectations.
Control.title: Description of Controls Suitability (ASAE 3150)
Control.evidence_required: System description document, control matrix, walkthroughs
Control.implementation_overview:
To implement the control CTL-057 under ASAE 3150 effectively, organizations in Australia must ensure that the description of internal controls is transparent, accurate, and meets the expectations set by assurance practitioners. This process involves meticulously documenting how controls are operationalized and ensuring alignment with relevant regulatory requirements, such as the Australian Privacy Principles and APRA guidelines. Organizations need a combination of manual documentation practices and automated tools to generate and maintain this control description to achieve a reliable and up-to-date system representation. This ensures that they comply with local laws while remaining clear on risk management tactics to support ongoing assurance engagements.

The implementation should involve various stakeholders, including compliance teams, IT departments, and audit practitioners, who will collectively ensure that the control description sufficiently covers all aspects of controls in place. Given the frequent changes in regulations and the need for continuous monitoring, a hybrid approach supports not only the effective documentation but also the adaptability to incorporate real-time updates as processes or regulatory requirements evolve. With Australia's increasing focus on cyber resilience and data privacy, this control becomes paramount, necessitating both strategic investment in technology and commitment to ongoing staff training and awareness.
common_pitfalls: Failure to engage relevant stakeholders during the documentation process, leading to incomplete descriptions.
Neglecting to update control descriptions regularly, resulting in documentation falling out of date.
Overlooking alignment with specific Australian regulations, such as APRA or privacy laws.
Control.manual_procedures: Implement manual workflows for situations where automation is not feasible; this involves creating templates for documentation, a checklist for compliance aspects, and a timeline for reviews. Ensure that these processes are documented and stored securely to align with APRA and ASD Essential Eight requirements.
Control.automation_details: Utilize compliance management systems (such as MetricStream or RSA Archer) that automate the documentation, monitoring, and assessment of controls, providing real-time visibility and updates. Additionally, leverage IAM tools like Okta to manage access control descriptions effectively.
Control.testing_and_validation: Conduct regular audits of the control descriptions against actual practices to ensure accuracy. Utilize checklists aligned with ASAE 3150 criteria and involve assurance practitioners in validation sessions.

Utilize a continuous improvement approach for control testing based on AUDIT findings.
Maintain detailed documentation and evidence for each testing cycle to ensure readiness for external audits.
Control.description: Controls operate effectively over the period under review; sufficient appropriate evidence available for testing.
Control.title: Operating Effectiveness Testing (ASAE 3150 Type 2)
Control.evidence_required: Sample evidence per control, testing workpapers, exception register
Control.implementation_overview:
To effectively implement Control CTL-058 under ASAE 3150, organizations in Australia must establish a robust framework that assures the ongoing effectiveness of controls over the review period. Given the complexities of the Australian regulatory environment, including compliance with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs), APRA requirements, and the Security of Critical Infrastructure (SOCI) Act, it is vital for organizations to leverage both manual and automated approaches. This hybrid model allows for comprehensive coverage, ensuring that control measures are not only in place but are also functioning as intended.

Implementation involves clearly defined roles and responsibilities among stakeholders, coupled with systematic testing and validation procedures. Regular monitoring and evidence collection are essential, and organizations should ensure that all activities align with the ASD’s Essential Eight practices, thus reinforcing resilience against cyber threats while adhering to international standards like ISO 27001. Moreover, clear documentation must be maintained to provide sufficient evidence during the audit processes, which can also support compliance audits by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA).
common_pitfalls: Insufficient documentation of testing procedures leading to non-compliance.
Neglecting to update control testing as organizational processes evolve or new threats emerge.
Failing to engage key stakeholders in the testing and validation process, which can lead to inaccurate results.
Control.manual_procedures: Establish detailed workflows ensuring that each manual control test aligns with documented procedures. Include steps for preparing control testing schedules, training stakeholders on procedures regarding the testing methods, and ensuring that findings are documented properly for compliance purposes. Procedures should be reflective of Australian Privacy Principles to ensure personal data handling and operational integrity.
Control.automation_details: Utilize automated testing tools like SIEM (e.g., Splunk) to continuously monitor controls and generate alerts for any irregularities. Additionally, leverage Identity and Access Management (IAM) solutions (e.g., Okta) for automating user access controls and compliance reporting. These tools will facilitate the timely detection of control failures and enhance reporting efficiency.
Control.testing_and_validation: Testing should focus on both manual and automated methods, analyzing historical data and conducting real-time monitoring for anomalies. Validation involves using established benchmarks from the ASAE 3150 framework and can include peer reviews of testing protocols and external audits to confirm the integrity of control operations.

implementation_steps.title: Implement Improvements

implementation_steps.title: Ongoing Monitoring and Review

Regularly review and update risk assessment procedures to remain aligned with current Australian standards and regulations.

Incorporate privacy impact assessments as part of the evaluation to protect personal data as mandated by the APRA.
Control.description: Sub-service organisations identified; carve-out or inclusive method documented with complementary user/sub-service controls.
Control.title: Subservice Organisation Carve-out / Inclusive Method (ASAE 3150)
Control.evidence_required: Subservice list, CUEC table, inclusive sub-service evidence
Control.implementation_overview: Implementing the Control ID CTL-059 under the ASAE 3150 framework necessitates a thorough understanding of how subservice organisations affect the service delivery and the associated controls. In Australian context, this requires compliance with the Australian Privacy Principles, APRA requirements, and the SOCI Act. The control can be approached either via a carve-out method, which excludes certain subservice organisation controls from the scope, or through the inclusive method, where all controls are accounted for in the engagement report. Documentation of complementary user and subservice controls is critical to ensure comprehensive oversight and alignment with regulatory bodies such as the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) through the Essential Eight framework, which emphasizes the protection of data and systems from cyber threats.
common_pitfalls: Failing to adequately assess and document all relevant complementary user controls leading to compliance gaps.
Neglecting to maintain updated records of the services provided by subservice organisations, resulting in outdated risk evaluations.
Overcomplicating the carve-out process, which may confuse stakeholders and hinder effective implementation.
Control.manual_procedures: For the hybrid approach, manual procedures might include quarterly reviews of the compliance documentation, direct communications with subservice organisations to collect performance metrics, and evidence of their compliance with key controls. Each of these processes should align with Australian Privacy Principles, ensuring that data handling is appropriate and risk is minimized.
Control.automation_details: Automated solutions like AWS IAM for identity management and Okta for access control can facilitate the oversight of subservice organisation interactions. Additionally, SIEM tools like Splunk can help in logging activities and detecting anomalies, ensuring compliance and security monitoring.
Control.testing_and_validation: To test and validate this control, conduct regular internal audits and assessments against the ASAE 3150 criteria. Use penetration testing on the interfaces between primary entities and subservice providers. Additionally, seek external compliance verification through independent third-party assessments.

Regularly train staff on CUECs and their importance
Incorporate user feedback into control updates
Utilize technology for monitoring and reporting compliance with CUECs
Control.description: Document and communicate complementary user entity controls required for the system to achieve its control objectives.
Control.title: Complementary User Entity Controls (CUECs) (ASAE 3150)
Control.evidence_required: CUEC schedule, customer notifications
Control.implementation_overview:
Implementing complementary user entity controls (CUECs) within the ASAE 3150 framework is crucial for ensuring that the security measures in place at subservice organizations are effectively complemented by user actions. In the Australian context, organizations must align these controls with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) as well as APRA requirements, ensuring that personal data is safeguarded while facilitating compliance with the SOCI Act and adhering to the ASD Essential Eight guidelines. 

The implementation of CUECs necessitates a collaborative effort between the service provider and user entities, where specific responsibilities and expectations are clearly defined. This involves documenting the necessary user entity controls and communicating these requirements effectively within the organization. Engaging stakeholders from both technical and governance aspects will enhance understanding and compliance, while ensuring that these controls are practical and aligned with organizational goals and regulatory requirements.
common_pitfalls: Failing to clearly communicate CUECs leading to confusion
Not aligning CUECs with actual practices within the organization
Neglecting to regularly review and update controls as regulations and technologies change
Control.manual_procedures: Manual procedures include preparing detailed documentation of CUECs, communicating these to stakeholders, and conducting training sessions. Specific workflows should be aligned with Australian regulations, ensuring that all user responsibilities are clear and that compliance is consistently monitored.
Control.automation_details: While some aspects of CUEC implementation can be automated, such as tracking user training completions and monitoring compliance through IAM and SIEM systems, much of the process relies on manual documentation and communication. Tools like AWS IAM can help manage user identities and permissions in relation to the controls.
Control.testing_and_validation: To validate the effectiveness of CUECs, organizations should conduct regular audits and assessments that include both automated tools and manual checks. Regular user feedback and testing scenarios can help confirm that the controls are functioning as intended and are well understood by all team members.

This should involve discussions with all stakeholders, including IT security teams and compliance officers.

implementation_steps.title: Implement Monitoring Mechanisms

Control.description: Maintain governance, policies, processes, controls and capabilities for managing information security risks across the CDR data environment.
Control.title: CDR Information Security Capability (CDR Schedule 2 Part 1)
Control.evidence_required: CDR ISMS, security capability statement, governance records
Control.implementation_overview: Implementing the CDR Information Security Capability requires a comprehensive approach that integrates both manual and automated processes to ensure the secure management of CDR data. This setup must address Australian-specific regulatory requirements under the Consumer Data Right (CDR), aligning with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) and standards such as ISO 27001. Organizations should start by establishing a governance framework that defines roles and responsibilities for managing information security within the CDR data environment.
common_pitfalls: Neglecting regular updates and reviews of information security policies.
Inadequate training leading to employee unawareness of security practices.
Failure to comply with the specific timelines and requirements set forth in the SOCI Act for incident reporting.
Control.manual_procedures: In addition to automated controls, develop manual procedures for complex processes such as risk assessment and incident response, ensuring detailed documentation of each step to facilitate compliance and audit requirements. Engage stakeholders in review processes to ensure comprehensive coverage.
Control.automation_details: Leverage automation tools such as Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems (e.g., Splunk, Sumo Logic) for real-time monitoring and reporting of information security events. Automate compliance checks using tools that can validate adherence to APRA requirements and CDR obligations.
Control.testing_and_validation: Validate this control by conducting penetration tests and simulated security breaches to assess response and resilience capabilities. Additionally, review the implementation of automated monitoring tools to ensure they generate accurate alerts and compliance reports.

Policies should be developed in line with the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) requirements and the Security of Critical Infrastructure (SOCI) Act.

Control.description: Clearly define and document the boundary of the CDR data environment, including systems, networks, personnel, vendors that interact with CDR data.
Control.title: CDR Data Environment Boundary Definition (CDR Schedule 2)
Control.evidence_required: Boundary diagram, scoping document, system inventory (CDR-tagged)
Control.implementation_overview:
Implementing the CDR Data Environment Boundary Definition control (CTL-062) requires organizations to systematically demarcate the boundaries of their CDR data environments, ensuring that all involved systems, networks, personnel, and vendors are clearly identified and documented. In Australia, this is crucial not just for compliance with the Consumer Data Right (CDR) framework, but also for alignment with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs), which emphasize the responsibility of organizations to manage personal information securely. Additionally, compliance with the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) requirements and the Security of Critical Infrastructure (SOCI) Act necessitates thorough delineation to protect sensitive consumer data from unauthorized access or breaches. 

The process involves not only identifying and documenting the relevant components of the CDR data environment but also regularly updating this documentation as systems or personnel change. Organisations should include considerations of their network architecture, vendor relationships, and personnel access to ensure comprehensive coverage. This implementation will help in conducting risk assessments and ensuring that security measures are appropriately applied in line with the ASD Essential Eight guidelines.
common_pitfalls: Failing to regularly update the boundary documentation leads to obsolete records.
Not involving all relevant stakeholders (IT, HR, Legal) in the boundary definition process.
Neglecting third-party vendors' data handling practices, which can expose the organization to risks.
Control.manual_procedures: The manual procedures entail thorough documentation practices, including the creation of living documents that are updated as changes occur within the data environment. Procedures should involve collaboration across departments to ensure comprehensive coverage of access and systems, with regular inputs from IT, HR, and compliance teams.
Control.automation_details: Due to the manual nature of this control, automation is limited. However, tools such as identity access management (IAM) systems can assist in managing access rights efficiently. Workflow management tools can also automate the documentation processes to some extent.
Control.testing_and_validation: Validation can be performed through internal audits and assessments to ensure all components within the CDR environment are documented correctly. Engage in tabletop exercises simulating data breaches to verify personnel's response and understanding of identified boundaries.

implementation_steps.description: List all systems, applications, networks, and databases where CDR data is stored, processed, or transmitted. This also includes backup systems. Maintain this list as a living document that is regularly updated.
implementation_steps.title: Identify CDR Data Components

implementation_steps.description: Document the roles of personnel who have access to the CDR data environment, including IT staff, data scientists, and management. Ensure there is a clear mapping of access rights based on roles.
implementation_steps.title: Document Personnel Access

implementation_steps.description: Identify all vendors and third parties that interact with CDR data, including subprocessors. Evaluate their compliance with CDR requirements and the potential risks they pose.
implementation_steps.title: Vendor and Third-Party Assessment

implementation_steps.description: Develop a formal documentation that defines the boundary of the CDR data environment, including visual aids such as diagrams to illustrate these boundaries clearly. Ensure this documentation is reviewed and approved by compliance officers.
implementation_steps.title: Define and Document Boundaries

implementation_steps.description: Establish a schedule for reviewing and updating the boundary definition documentation at least annually or when significant changes occur in systems or personnel.
implementation_steps.title: Conduct Regular Reviews and Updates

implementation_steps.description: Develop a training program for relevant personnel to ensure they are aware of the boundaries defined for the CDR data environment and their responsibilities in protecting this data.
implementation_steps.title: Training and Awareness

Control.description: Strong authentication (MFA), least-privilege access controls, privileged access management for personnel accessing CDR data.
Control.title: CDR Access Controls and Authentication (CDR Schedule 2 Part 1 Step 4)
Control.evidence_required: MFA enforcement, PAM logs, JML records for CDR access
Control.implementation_overview:
Implementing CDR Access Controls and Authentication (CTL-063) in Australia is essential for ensuring the security of consumer data under the Consumer Data Right (CDR) legislation. Organizations must establish strong authentication mechanisms, such as Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and employ least-privilege access controls to protect consumer data from unauthorized access. This control aligns with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) as well as the Australian Signals Directorate's (ASD) Essential Eight strategies to mitigate cybersecurity risks. The integration of both manual and automated processes allows organizations to tailor their approach according to their specific operational and risk environments, thereby ensuring compliance with both regulatory requirements and industry best practices.

Organizations should continuously monitor access to CDR data, implement privileged access management strategies, and provide ongoing training to staff regarding data handling practices. Regular audits and assessments based on APRA requirements are recommended to verify compliance and measure the effectiveness of the controls in place, ultimately contributing to the protection of consumers’ rights.
common_pitfalls: Neglecting regular reviews of access privileges leads to accumulated unnecessary access rights.
Failing to enforce MFA across all components handling CDR data.
Underestimating the importance of user training on secure data handling practices.
Control.manual_procedures: Organizations must maintain detailed documentation of user access approvals, including specific justifications, which should be reviewed periodically in line with APRA requirements. Manual processes should also include incident response procedures that outline steps for handling unauthorized access events, ensuring they are communicated effectively to all relevant stakeholders.
Control.automation_details: Utilizing Identity and Access Management (IAM) systems like Okta or Microsoft Azure AD can automate user provisioning, de-provisioning, and the enforcement of MFA. Additionally, SIEM tools like Splunk can be leveraged to monitor access logs and detect anomalies in real-time, enhancing security posture.
Control.testing_and_validation: Validation of access controls should involve simulated unauthorized access attempts to test the efficacy of MFA and privilege management. Regular penetration testing should be undertaken to identify vulnerabilities in the authentication process. Additionally, reviews of user activity logs should ensure that the appropriate access controls are in place and functioning as expected.

implementation_steps.description: Define strong authentication requirements tailored to CDR access, ensuring compliance with Australian Privacy Principles and relevant legislation.
implementation_steps.title: Establish Authentication Requirements

implementation_steps.description: Deploy MFA across all systems handling CDR data to ensure that users authenticate through at least two independent verification methods.
implementation_steps.title: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication

implementation_steps.description: Create and document policies enforcing least-privilege access controls, ensuring users only have access necessary for their roles.
implementation_steps.title: Define Access Control Policies

implementation_steps.title: Deploy Privileged Access Management Solutions

implementation_steps.title: User Training and Awareness

implementation_steps.description: Set up continuous monitoring mechanisms to detect unauthorized access attempts and report incidents as per SOCI Act requirements.
implementation_steps.title: Monitoring and Reporting

implementation_steps.description: Perform regular audits to ensure compliance with CDR access control policies and assess the effectiveness of authentication mechanisms.
implementation_steps.title: Regular Audits and Reviews

Conduct regular training for employees regarding data protection best practices
Implement multi-factor authentication for systems handling encryption keys
Engage in regular risk assessments and penetration testing to identify vulnerabilities
Stay updated with CDR and APP regulatory developments to adjust practices accordingly
Control.description: CDR data must be encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES-256 or equivalent); key management aligned to ACCC/OAIC expectations.
Control.title: CDR Data Encryption in Transit and At Rest (CDR Schedule 2)
Control.evidence_required: TLS configs, KMS records, encryption attestations
Control.implementation_overview:
Implementing the CDR Data Encryption Control (CTL-064) entails ensuring that Consumer Data Right (CDR) data is securely encrypted both in transit and at rest, in line with the ACCC and OAIC guidelines. For Australian organisations handling CDR data, compliance is paramount not only for customer trust but also for adherence to the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) and the APRA requirements. This control mandates the use of TLS 1.2 or higher for data in transit, ensuring that any data exchanged over networks is safeguarded against interception and unauthorized access.

At rest, CDR data must utilize AES-256 encryption or an equivalent standard, with comprehensive key management practices that align with the Australian Cyber Security Centre's Essential Eight strategies. Implementing this control effectively requires a blend of automated encryption solutions where feasible, supplemented by robust manual oversight to ensure that encryption keys are properly managed and audited. An organisation should consider engaging in a thorough risk assessment to understand the potential vulnerabilities in their data handling processes and to implement this control accordingly, ensuring both large enterprises and mid-market organisations are effectively protected.
common_pitfalls: Underestimating the complexity of key management processes
Failing to consistently enforce TLS 1.2 or higher across all systems
Neglecting to document data flows and encryption practices
Inadequate staff training on encryption policies and access control measures
Lack of performance monitoring for encryption efficacy
Control.manual_procedures: Manual processes must include detailed, documented procedures for key management, including physical security measures for key storage and access, complemented by regular reviews to ensure compliance with ACCC/OAIC guidelines. This may include manual logging of encryption key changes, maintenance of a key access list, and securing backup copies of encrypted data following APRA requirements.
Control.automation_details: Tools like AWS KMS for key management, automated encryption services in cloud environments, and SIEM tools like Splunk can assist in automating encryption practices and auditing processes. Additionally, TLS configuration tools can further automate the enforcement of encryption standards across data transmission points.
Control.testing_and_validation: Testing can be carried out through automated vulnerability assessments and regular penetration tests to confirm that encryption standards are properly implemented and effective. Additionally, routine audits of key management practices and regular reviews of encrypted data access logs can aid in validating compliance with the control.

implementation_steps.description: Conduct a thorough assessment of all data flows involving CDR data to identify where encryption is required both in transit and at rest. Document these data flows to guide implementation efforts and ensure compliance with the APPs and the CDR framework.
implementation_steps.title: Assess and Document Data Flows

implementation_steps.description: Ensure that all data transmissions use TLS 1.2 or higher. This may involve configuring web servers, API gateways, and any intermediary systems to enforce TLS encryption for all data transfers involving CDR data.

implementation_steps.description: Implement AES-256 encryption or an equivalent standard for all CDR data stored on digital storage systems. This includes databases, cloud storage solutions, and any backups containing CDR data.
implementation_steps.title: Encrypt Data at Rest

implementation_steps.description: Develop and implement key management policies that are in line with ACCC/OAIC expectations. This includes generating, storing, rotating, and retiring encryption keys securely and ensuring access controls are in place for key management processes.
implementation_steps.title: Establish Key Management Practices

implementation_steps.description: Provide training for all relevant staff on the encryption policies and procedures, changes to data handling processes, and reporting measures for any suspected breaches regarding CDR data.
implementation_steps.title: Train Staff on Encryption Policies

implementation_steps.description: Set up ongoing monitoring to ensure compliance with encryption standards and perform regular audits to verify that CDR data is properly encrypted in transit and at rest as per the documented data flows. Review audit findings and adjust practices as necessary.
implementation_steps.title: Monitor and Audit Encryption Practices

best_practices: Conduct routine training for staff on privacy obligations under the Privacy Act 1988 and CDR guidelines.
Integrate PIAs into all project management processes as standard practice for projects involving personal data.
Maintain an up-to-date register of PIAs conducted and their outcomes for governance and compliance tracking.
Control.description: Conduct PIA prior to commencing or materially changing CDR data handling; address compliance with Privacy Safeguards.
Control.title: CDR Privacy Impact Assessment (Privacy Safeguard 1)
Control.evidence_required: PIA reports, sign-off, remediation actions
Control.implementation_overview:
To implement Control ID CTL-065 effectively in the Australian context, organizations must conduct a thorough Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA) whenever they initiate or significantly alter their handling of Consumer Data Right (CDR) data. This ensures compliance with Australian Privacy Principle (APP) 1 and aligns with the requirements set forth by the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC). Organizations need to establish governance frameworks that embed PIA processes into their project planning methodologies. This should involve collaboration between legal, compliance, and IT teams to address potential privacy risks and ensure that data handling practices conform to the latest regulatory standards, including the Privacy Act and the Consumer Data Right legislation. 

The process should be transparent and well-documented, making it easier to demonstrate compliance to regulatory bodies during audits. Organizations can also reference the Australian Signals Directorate (ASD) Essential Eight guidelines to strengthen their data protection measures while performing PIAs, ensuring that any new projects involving CDR data are assessed for potential privacy risks before their commencement and monitored continuously throughout their lifecycle.
common_pitfalls: Failing to conduct a PIA at the right time, leading to non-compliance risks.
Inadequately documenting risks and mitigation strategies, risking future liability.
Neglecting stakeholder consultation, which can lead to oversights in addressing privacy concerns.
Control.frequency: at the commencement of each new project or material change in CDR data handling practices
Control.manual_procedures: The PIA process includes gathering input from relevant stakeholders during workshops, utilizing templates for consistency in reporting, and ensuring that all documentation is stored securely and is easily accessible for audits. This manual procedure should also detail the steps for consulting affected parties when necessary to obtain their insights on privacy concerns.
Control.automation_details: Automation may involve using software tools for risk assessment and management dashboards to track compliance status over time. Solutions like Privacy Impact Assessment Management Tools, which offer automated workflows, risk scoring, and documentation management, could be integrated. Additionally, using platforms like Microsoft Azure or Google Cloud for centralized data management can streamline compliance tracking.
Control.testing_and_validation: To test and validate the PIA process, organizations should simulate data handling scenarios to assess whether the identified risks and mitigation strategies are effective. Regular audits of completed PIAs and their implementation should be conducted to ensure compliance with APPs and internal policies, supplemented by feedback from stakeholders involving in the data handling processes.

implementation_steps.description: Determine if the proposed project involves the handling of CDR data and identify scenarios that would require a PIA. Document the nature and scope of the data handling, including any significant changes to existing processes.
implementation_steps.title: Identify the CDR Data Handling Changes

implementation_steps.description: Perform an initial analysis to categorize the type and sensitivity of CDR data involved in the proposed project. This includes assessing risks related to data breaches, unauthorized access, and impacts on consumer privacy.

implementation_steps.description: Create a comprehensive PIA report outlining the identified privacy risks, the impact of data handling changes, and proposed mitigation strategies. Align the report with relevant Australian regulatory frameworks, including APPs and APRA guidelines.
implementation_steps.title: Develop the Privacy Impact Assessment Report

implementation_steps.description: Submit the PIA report for review by senior management and, if required, seek input from legal experts to ensure all compliance obligations are met. Obtain formal approvals to proceed based on risk assessment outcomes.
implementation_steps.title: Review and Approve the PIA

implementation_steps.description: Put in place necessary controls and processes to mitigate identified risks before commencing or altering CDR data handling. This may involve technical controls, policy updates, or training initiatives.
implementation_steps.title: Implement Risk Mitigation Strategies

implementation_steps.description: Establish ongoing monitoring mechanisms to ensure that CDR data handling continues to align with the PIA findings and follows privacy safeguards. Regularly review the effectiveness of implemented controls.
implementation_steps.title: Monitor and Review Data Handling Practices

implementation_steps.description: After completing the PIA process, document lessons learned and update organizational policies and procedures to reflect any changes or improvements identified during the assessment.
implementation_steps.title: Document Lessons Learned

best_practices: Design the consent dashboard for clarity and user-friendliness according to UX principles to enhance consumer engagement.
Ensure that records of consent are timestamped and securely stored to comply with APRA requirements.
Implement regular audits and assessments of consent practices to align with ISO27001 standards.
Provide transparency about how consent is used and the implications of withdrawal, adhering to the Australian Privacy Principles.
Control.description: Express, voluntary, time-limited, specific consent obtained from CDR consumers; consent dashboard with withdrawal mechanism within standards.
Control.title: CDR Consumer Consent Management (Privacy Safeguard 3, CDR Rules)
Control.evidence_required: Consent records, dashboard screenshots, withdrawal logs
Control.implementation_overview:
Implementing CDR Consumer Consent Management is crucial for compliance with Privacy Safeguard 3 under the Consumer Data Right (CDR) framework in Australia. Organizations need to ensure they obtain express, voluntary, time-limited, and specific consent from consumers for the collection, use, and disclosure of their data. A well-designed consent management system must provide a user-friendly consent dashboard that enables consumers to view and withdraw their consent easily. Given the sensitive nature of personal information, it is essential to align the implementation with Australian Privacy Principles (APPs) and the Australian Government’s data protection standards, ensuring that all data handling practices uphold consumer rights and privacy protections.

For organizations, especially those in the financial and telecommunications sectors, the setup may involve both manual processes to comply with certain regulatory requirements and automated solutions to handle consent efficiently. This dual approach allows organizations to manage consent effectively while ensuring consumer trust and compliance with the Australian Privacy Act, APRA guidelines, and the ongoing vigilance of the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) Essential Eight strategies.
common_pitfalls: Failing to update consent processes in line with changes to regulations or consumer expectations.
Inadequate training for staff leading to inconsistent consumer interactions regarding consent.
Overcomplicating the consent withdrawal process, possibly deterring consumers from engaging with the platform.
Neglecting to audit consent records and monitor compliance over time, leading to potential breaches.
Control.manual_procedures: Manual oversight will be necessary during training sessions to ensure staff effectively communicate consent rights and during audits to confirm that consumer consent is documented correctly. Ensure periodic reviews of consent records are conducted manually to validate compliance with Privacy Safeguard 3.
Control.automation_details: Utilize cloud-based consent management platforms such as AWS Cognito for identity management and consent tracking, and deploy analytics platforms like Google Analytics to monitor consumer interactions. Integration with other systems (like CRM) will automate the flow of consent data across your operational frameworks.
Control.testing_and_validation: Conduct functional testing of the consent management system, ensuring all user scenarios (such as giving, viewing, and withdrawing consent) work seamlessly. Perform security testing to validate that consent records are protected and conduct user acceptance testing (UAT) by engaging real users for feedback.

implementation_steps.description: Develop a framework that outlines how consent will be collected, managed, and documented. This includes identifying data types, consent requirements, and time limits for data use, ensuring adherence to the CDR Rules and APPs.
implementation_steps.title: Design Consent Framework

implementation_steps.description: Create an automated system to capture and store consumer consent, which includes a dashboard for consumers to manage their preferences easily. The system should comply with CDR standards, allowing explicit consent and enabling withdrawal of consent at any time.
implementation_steps.title: Develop Consent Management System

implementation_steps.description: Develop and conduct training for staff to understand consent requirements and how to assist consumers in managing their consent preferences. This ensures all employees are aware of CDR compliance obligations.
implementation_steps.title: Implement Training Program

implementation_steps.description: Conduct thorough testing of the consent management system to ensure it operates correctly and meets regulatory requirements. Validate that consumers can effectively give and withdraw consent through the dashboard without issues.
implementation_steps.title: Testing and Validation

implementation_steps.description: Establish procedures for the ongoing monitoring of consent management processes, including regular audits to ensure compliance with any updates to CDR regulations or Australian privacy laws. Update procedures based on regulatory changes or consumer feedback.
implementation_steps.title: Ongoing Monitoring and Updates

tools_and_systems: AWS Cognito for consent and identity management
Okta for user authentication and single sign-on
Splunk for monitoring consent management system activities
Google Analytics for tracking user interactions with consent mechanisms

Risk.treatment_plan: Consent dashboard aligned to CX Standards; legal review; audit trail (CTL-066)
Risk.description: Consent not express, voluntary, time-limited or specific; or not properly recorded; breach of CDR Rules and PS3.
Risk.title: CDR Consumer Consent Failure / Invalid Consent

Risk.treatment_plan: Recipient verification against ACCC register; technical access controls; audit logs
Risk.description: CDR data disclosed to a party that is not properly accredited or authorised by consumer; breach of PS6.
Risk.title: CDR Data Disclosure to Unaccredited Recipient

Risk.treatment_plan: Clear boundary definition; data flow mapping; DLP at boundary; quarterly scoping review (CTL-062)
Risk.description: CDR data leaks into systems outside defined CDR boundary, increasing attack surface and undermining controls.
Risk.title: CDR Boundary Scope Creep

Risk.treatment_plan: Automated retention schedule; deletion/de-identification jobs; verification logs (CTL-068)
Risk.description: CDR data retained beyond consent / statutory period in breach of PS11/12 and CDR Rules.
Risk.title: CDR Data Retained Beyond Permitted Period

Risk.treatment_plan: Badge access, visitor escort, CCTV, clean desk (CTL-026, CTL-048)
Risk.description: Unauthorised physical access to data centres, offices or device storage leads to data theft or sabotage.
Risk.title: Physical Access Compromise of Sensitive Areas

Risk.treatment_plan: Compliance register; legal review; obligation tracking with owners and evidence (CTL-023, CTL-045)
Risk.description: Breach of legal, regulatory or contractual security obligations resulting in fines, lawsuits or contract loss.
Risk.title: Regulatory or Contractual Non-Compliance

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement CTL-001 control with regional enforcement and quarterly audits
Risk.description: Customer data stored in non-compliant regions due to cloud configuration errors or unavailability
Risk.title: Data Residency Non-Compliance

Risk.treatment_plan: Enforce responsibility via OBL-001 and conduct annual SOC 2 reviews
Risk.description: Cloud provider fails to meet security responsibilities due to unclear definitions
Risk.title: Inadequate Cloud Provider Accountability

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement CTL-003 network segregation and annual pen testing
Risk.description: Unauthorized access between customer environments in multi-tenant cloud infrastructure
Risk.title: Cross-Tenant Data Breach

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement CTL-002 exit procedures and annual DR exercises
Risk.description: Unable to retrieve data or maintain services when changing cloud providers
Risk.title: Cloud Provider Exit Failure

Risk.treatment_plan: Enforce DPA restrictions via CTL-007 with annual compliance audits
Risk.description: Cloud provider using customer personal data for own purposes or profit
Risk.title: Unauthorized Personal Data Use by Cloud Provider

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement CTL-009 breach procedures with notification templates and automation
Risk.description: Failure to notify data subjects and authorities within required timeframes
Risk.title: Personal Data Breach Notification Failure

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement CTL-008 data access portal with automated data export
Risk.description: Cannot provide personal data to data subjects or in portable format within 30 days
Risk.title: Inability to Meet Data Subject Access Requests

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement CTL-006 retention and deletion controls with automated enforcement
Risk.description: Personal data retained beyond required period or not securely deleted
Risk.title: Personal Data Retention Violations

Risk.treatment_plan: Enforce sub-processor approval via OBL-010 with quarterly audits
Risk.description: Cloud provider engaging unauthorized sub-processors to process personal data
Risk.title: Unauthorized Sub-Processor Usage

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement encryption, access controls, and breach procedures via CTL-009
Risk.description: Unauthorized access to personal data stored in cloud services affecting data subjects
Risk.title: Personal Data Breach in Cloud Environment

Risk.treatment_plan: Implement responsibility matrix via CTL-001 and enforce responsibility reviews
Risk.description: Ambiguity between customer and cloud provider security responsibilities leading to security gaps
Risk.title: Unclear Cloud Security Responsibilities

Policy.description: Establishes the methodology for identifying, assessing, treating and monitoring information security risks across the ISMS.
Policy.title: Risk Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Risk Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines the organisation's approach to identifying, assessing, treating, monitoring and reporting information security risks. It establishes the methodology and governance structures necessary to maintain an Information Security Management System (ISMS) aligned with ISO/IEC 27001 Clauses 6.1 and 8, the SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (CC3.1–CC3.4, CC9), the ASAE 3150 assurance framework, and the Competition and Consumer (Consumer Data Right) Rules 2020.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all information assets, business processes, personnel, systems, third parties and physical locations within the ISMS scope, including the Consumer Data Right (CDR) data environment. It covers strategic, operational, technical, third-party, legal/regulatory and physical risks that may affect the confidentiality, integrity or availability of information.

3. Risk Management Principles

  • Risk-based decision-making: Security investment shall be proportionate to the assessed risk;
  • Documented and repeatable: The methodology shall be applied consistently across the organisation;
  • Owned and accountable: Every risk shall have a single accountable owner;
  • Continual improvement: The risk register and methodology shall be reviewed and improved over time;
  • Aligned with business objectives: Risk treatment shall support the achievement of strategic and regulatory objectives.

4. Risk Assessment Methodology

4.1 Risk Identification

Risks shall be identified through structured workshops, threat modelling, vulnerability assessments, audit findings, incident reviews, supplier assurance, regulatory horizon scanning, and ongoing monitoring of the threat landscape.

4.2 Risk Analysis and Evaluation

Each risk shall be analysed using a Likelihood (1–5) x Impact (1–5) scoring matrix against documented criteria covering financial, operational, regulatory and reputational impact. The resulting risk score shall be used to determine treatment priority and management approval thresholds.

4.3 Risk Treatment

Treatment options are: mitigate, transfer, avoid or accept. Where a control is selected, it shall be mapped to ISO 27001 Annex A and recorded in the Statement of Applicability (SoA). Residual risk shall be re-scored after treatment.

4.4 Risk Acceptance

Residual risks above the agreed appetite shall require formal written acceptance by the executive accountable owner and, for High/Critical residual risks, the CEO. Acceptance shall be time-bound and reviewed at least annually.

5. Risk Register

A central risk register shall be maintained capturing for each risk: identifier, title, description, category, owner, likelihood, impact, score, treatment, treatment plan, target date, residual score, status and linked controls/obligations. The register shall be reviewed quarterly by the Security Steering Committee.

6. Risk Appetite

The Board shall approve and annually review the organisation's risk appetite statement, expressed in terms of acceptable likelihood and impact ranges per risk category. The CISO shall report exposure against appetite at each Management Review.

7. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Board of DirectorsApprove risk appetite; oversee risk posture
CEOApprove High/Critical residual risk acceptance
CISOOwn methodology; maintain risk register; report quarterly
Risk OwnersAccountable for treatment plan execution within agreed timeline
Security Steering CommitteeQuarterly review of register and treatment progress
Internal AuditIndependent assurance over the risk management process

8. Frequency and Triggers

A formal enterprise risk assessment shall be performed at least annually. Targeted reassessments shall be triggered by: significant organisational or technology change, material incident, new regulatory obligation, significant supplier change, or audit finding.

9. Compliance

Non-compliance with this policy may result in disciplinary action and shall be reported to the CISO. Findings shall feed into the corrective action process.

10. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually by the CISO and approved by the CEO.

11. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Clauses 6.1, 8.2, 8.3 and Annex A.5.4; SOC 2 CC3.1–CC3.4, CC9.1–CC9.2; ASAE 3150 control objectives for risk management; and CDR Privacy Safeguard 12 (Schedule 2 Part 1).

Policy.description: Defines acceptable use of information assets and systems by personnel and contractors.
Policy.title: Acceptable Use Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Acceptable Use Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the rules and responsibilities for the acceptable use of the organisation's information systems, networks, devices and data. It protects the organisation, its personnel, customers and partners from harms arising from inappropriate or unauthorised use.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all employees, contractors, consultants, temporary workers, interns and any other persons granted access to the organisation's information assets, regardless of location. It covers organisation-owned and personally-owned devices used to access organisation systems or data.

3. General Principles

  • Use of organisation systems is permitted for legitimate business purposes; limited and reasonable personal use is permitted provided it does not interfere with duties or breach this policy;
  • All activity on organisation systems may be logged, monitored and audited in accordance with applicable law;
  • Users shall not have an expectation of privacy when using organisation systems;
  • Users are personally accountable for activity performed under their accounts.

4. Permitted Use

4.1 Authorised Activities

  • Performing assigned business duties using organisation-approved systems and software;
  • Communicating with colleagues, customers and approved third parties using organisation channels;
  • Accessing approved cloud and SaaS services for business purposes.

4.2 Account and Credential Use

  • Each user shall use only their own uniquely identified account;
  • Credentials shall not be shared, written down in unsecured locations, or stored in browsers without an approved password manager;
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) shall not be bypassed or shared.

5. Prohibited Use

  • Installing unauthorised, pirated or unlicensed software;
  • Connecting unauthorised devices or removable media to organisation networks;
  • Disabling, bypassing or interfering with security controls (e.g. EDR, DLP, encryption);
  • Sending organisation data to personal email, personal cloud storage or unauthorised messaging apps;
  • Accessing material that is illegal, harassing, discriminatory or sexually explicit;
  • Conducting personal commercial activity, gambling, or activity that breaches law or contract;
  • Use of generative AI tools with sensitive, customer or CDR data without an approved exception;
  • Connecting to untrusted networks without VPN/ZTNA.

6. Device Security

  • All devices accessing organisation data shall be enrolled in the organisation's endpoint management and EDR solution;
  • Full-disk encryption is mandatory; screens shall lock after no more than 10 minutes of inactivity;
  • Operating systems and software shall be kept up to date in accordance with the patching SLA;
  • Lost or stolen devices shall be reported to IT and the CISO immediately.

7. Communications

  • Email and messaging shall be used professionally and in accordance with classification handling requirements;
  • Sensitive information shall not be transmitted via unapproved channels;
  • External-facing content shall not disclose confidential or restricted information.

8. Workspace and Physical

  • Clear desk and clear screen practices shall be observed;
  • Visitors shall not be left unattended in restricted areas;
  • Printed sensitive information shall be securely stored and securely destroyed when no longer required.

9. Reporting Obligations

Users shall promptly report suspected security incidents, phishing attempts, lost devices, accidental data disclosure, or breaches of this policy to the CISO via the incident reporting channel.

10. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
All UsersComply with this policy; report incidents
Line ManagersReinforce policy; address breaches
HREmbed policy in onboarding; manage disciplinary process
IT / SecurityImplement technical controls; monitor for misuse
CISOOwn and maintain this policy

11. Compliance and Enforcement

Breach of this policy may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination of employment or contract, and may give rise to civil or criminal liability. Significant or repeat breaches shall be reported to the CISO.

12. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually by the CISO and approved by the CEO.

13. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.10, A.6.2, A.6.3, A.7.7, A.8.1, A.8.7; SOC 2 CC1.1, CC1.4, CC2.2, CC6.1; and CDR Privacy Safeguard 12.

Policy.description: Classifies information based on sensitivity and prescribes handling, labelling, transmission and disposal requirements.
Policy.title: Data Classification and Handling Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Data Classification and Handling Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Data Governance  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines how information is classified by sensitivity and the corresponding requirements for labelling, handling, transmission, storage, retention and disposal. Consistent classification is the foundation for proportionate, evidence-based information protection.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all information assets created, received, processed, stored or transmitted by the organisation in any format (digital, physical, verbal), and to all personnel and third parties handling such information. Special requirements apply to Consumer Data Right (CDR) data and personal information regulated under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth).

3. Classification Levels

LevelDefinitionExamples
PublicInformation approved for public disclosure with no harm if disclosedMarketing material, public website content
InternalInformation for internal business use; limited harm if disclosedInternal procedures, organisation charts
ConfidentialSensitive business information; significant harm if disclosedCustomer data, financials, source code, contracts
RestrictedHighly sensitive or regulated information; severe harm if disclosedCDR data, personal information, payment card data, authentication secrets, encryption keys

4. Classification Responsibilities

  • The Information Owner is accountable for classifying information assets they own;
  • All personnel are responsible for handling information according to its classification;
  • If classification is uncertain, the higher level shall be applied until reviewed;
  • Inherited data (e.g. derived datasets) inherits the highest classification of its sources.

5. Labelling Requirements

  • Confidential and Restricted documents shall display the classification in headers/footers;
  • Email containing Confidential/Restricted information shall use the approved classification banner;
  • System datasets containing Restricted information shall be tagged in the data catalogue;
  • Public and Internal materials may be left unlabelled where context makes the level clear.

6. Handling Requirements

6.1 Storage

  • Public/Internal: approved corporate systems;
  • Confidential: approved corporate systems with access controls; encryption at rest;
  • Restricted: dedicated systems within defined boundary (CDR boundary for CDR data); encryption at rest with managed keys; access logging.

6.2 Transmission

  • All Confidential and Restricted data shall be transmitted using TLS 1.2 or higher;
  • Restricted data shall not be sent via personal channels, unencrypted email, or consumer file sharing;
  • Bulk export of Restricted data requires prior approval from the Data Owner and CISO.

6.3 Access

  • Access shall be granted on a need-to-know basis under the Access Control Policy;
  • Restricted data access shall be MFA-enforced and reviewed at least quarterly.

6.4 Printing and Physical

  • Restricted data shall not be printed unless strictly required and shall be securely destroyed (cross-cut shred or secure bin) immediately after use.

7. Retention and Disposal

Information shall be retained only for as long as required by business need, contract, or law, and shall then be securely deleted or de-identified. CDR data shall be deleted or de-identified in accordance with CDR Privacy Safeguards 11 and 12 and the Retention Schedule. Disposal of physical media shall use NIST SP 800-88 compliant methods.

8. CDR Data Specific Requirements

  • All CDR data is classified Restricted by default;
  • CDR data shall only be processed within the documented CDR data environment boundary;
  • CDR data shall not be combined with non-CDR data outside approved use cases;
  • Disclosure to third parties shall be permitted only where consistent with consumer consent and CDR Rules.

9. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Data GovernanceOwns this policy; maintains classification scheme
Information OwnersClassify owned information; approve access
CISOApproves controls and exceptions for Restricted data
All UsersHandle data according to classification

10. Compliance

Non-compliance may lead to disciplinary action and shall be reported to the CISO. Material incidents shall be assessed against breach notification obligations.

11. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually by Data Governance and approved by the CISO.

12. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.9–A.5.13, A.5.34, A.8.10–A.8.12; SOC 2 CC6.1, CC6.5, C1.1, C1.2; ISO/IEC 27018; and CDR Privacy Safeguards 11 and 12.

Policy.description: Defines the framework for detecting, responding to, recovering from and learning from security incidents.
Policy.title: Incident Response Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Incident Response Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Incident Response Team  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the framework for the timely, effective and consistent detection, response, recovery and post-incident review of information security incidents. It supports compliance with ISO/IEC 27001, SOC 2, ASAE 3150, the Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth), and the CDR Rules eligible data breach obligations.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all information security incidents affecting the organisation's people, systems, data, customers, suppliers or services, including events affecting CDR data, personal information or critical operations.

3. Definitions

  • Event: An observable occurrence in a system or network;
  • Security Incident: An event that has caused or has the potential to cause a breach of confidentiality, integrity or availability;
  • Eligible Data Breach (NDB): Unauthorised access/disclosure or loss of personal information likely to result in serious harm;
  • CDR Eligible Data Breach: Unauthorised access, disclosure or loss of CDR data per CDR Rules s 56.

4. Incident Response Phases

4.1 Preparation

  • Maintain documented playbooks for top scenarios (ransomware, BEC, credential theft, DDoS, third-party breach, CDR breach);
  • Maintain trained Incident Response Team (IRT) with on-call coverage;
  • Conduct tabletop exercise at least annually with documented learnings.

4.2 Identification and Triage

  • Detection sources include SIEM/EDR alerts, user reports, third-party notifications, and threat intelligence;
  • Triage shall classify severity within the response time SLA below.

4.3 Containment

Short-term containment to limit damage (e.g. host isolation, credential rotation, blocking IOCs) shall be performed before evidence collection where required, with chain-of-custody preserved for forensics.

4.4 Eradication

Root cause shall be identified and remediated. Affected systems shall be rebuilt or sanitised before being returned to service.

4.5 Recovery

Restoration of services shall be validated, monitoring intensified, and customer/business impact communicated as appropriate.

4.6 Lessons Learned

A post-incident review shall be conducted within 14 calendar days of closure for Sev1/Sev2, with corrective actions tracked to completion.

5. Severity Classification and SLAs

SeverityDescriptionInitial ResponseExecutive Notification
Sev1Critical breach, major outage, regulated data compromise15 minutes1 hour
Sev2Significant impact; partial service or limited data exposure1 hour4 hours
Sev3Limited impact, no regulated data exposure4 hoursDaily summary
Sev4Minor / informationalNext business dayWeekly summary

6. Notification Obligations

  • NDB Scheme (Privacy Act): Notify OAIC and affected individuals as soon as practicable where an eligible data breach is identified;
  • CDR Eligible Data Breach: Notify ACCC, OAIC and affected consumers in accordance with CDR Rules s 56 and the breach response playbook;
  • Customers: Notify per contractual SLAs and assurance commitments;
  • Regulators / Law Enforcement: Engage as required by jurisdiction.

7. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Incident CommanderOverall coordination of response
CISOExecutive accountability; regulator engagement
DPO / Privacy OfficerPrivacy assessment; NDB / CDR breach decisions
LegalLegal advice; privilege; regulator liaison
CommunicationsInternal and external messaging
IRT EngineersTechnical containment, eradication, recovery
HRInsider-related incidents

8. Evidence Handling

All evidence shall be collected, transported, stored and destroyed under documented chain-of-custody. Forensic copies shall be made before remediation where feasible.

9. Testing and Improvement

The IRT shall conduct at least one tabletop exercise per year and one technical simulation per year. Findings shall be tracked through to closure and feed into policy and playbook updates.

10. Compliance

Failure to follow this policy may impair the organisation's ability to meet legal and regulatory obligations and may result in disciplinary action.

11. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually and after every Sev1 incident.

12. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.24–A.5.28, A.6.8; SOC 2 CC7.3–CC7.5; ASAE 3150 incident management control objectives; the NDB scheme under the Privacy Act 1988; and CDR Rules s 56.

Policy.description: Defines requirements for ensuring continuity of operations and recovery from disasters.
Policy.title: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Business Continuity Manager  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the framework for ensuring continuity of critical business operations and the recovery of information systems following disruptive events. It supports the resilience commitments made to customers, regulators and stakeholders, and aligns with ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.5.29–A.5.30 and SOC 2 Availability criteria (A1.1–A1.3).

2. Scope

This policy applies to all critical business processes, supporting information systems, third-party dependencies, premises and personnel necessary to deliver the organisation's products and services, including services within the Consumer Data Right (CDR) data environment.

3. Objectives

  • Maintain continuity of critical services within agreed Recovery Time Objectives (RTO);
  • Limit data loss to within agreed Recovery Point Objectives (RPO);
  • Protect personnel safety as the highest priority during disruption;
  • Meet regulatory and contractual continuity obligations;
  • Continuously improve resilience based on tests and incidents.

4. Business Impact Analysis (BIA)

A BIA shall be performed at least annually and following material change. The BIA shall identify critical business processes, supporting assets, dependencies, maximum tolerable period of disruption, RTO, RPO and minimum business continuity objective for each.

5. Recovery Targets

Service TierRTORPO
Tier 1 (Critical / customer-facing / CDR)≤ 4 hours≤ 15 minutes
Tier 2 (Important)≤ 24 hours≤ 4 hours
Tier 3 (Standard)≤ 72 hours≤ 24 hours
Tier 4 (Non-essential)≤ 7 days≤ 7 days

6. Architecture and Controls

  • Critical systems shall be deployed across multiple availability zones with automated failover;
  • Backups shall be encrypted, geographically separated and immutable where supported;
  • Capacity planning shall ensure peak load can be sustained;
  • DDoS protection, WAF and edge resilience shall be in place for internet-facing services.

7. Plans

  • Business Continuity Plan (BCP): Defines manual workarounds, alternate locations and personnel arrangements;
  • Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP): Defines technical procedures for system recovery;
  • Crisis Communications Plan: Defines internal/external communications during disruption.

8. Testing

  • BCP tabletop exercise: at least annually;
  • DR technical recovery test for Tier 1 systems: at least annually with documented success against RTO/RPO;
  • Backup restore test: at least quarterly for Tier 1 data;
  • Test results, deviations and corrective actions shall be documented and tracked.

9. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
CEOApprove BCP strategy and risk appetite
Business Continuity ManagerOwn programme, BIA, plans and testing
Service OwnersMaintain DR runbooks for owned systems
CISOEnsure security of recovery and continuity controls
HR / FacilitiesPeople and premises continuity

10. Suppliers and Third Parties

Continuity dependencies on suppliers (including subservice organisations under ASAE 3150) shall be documented, and contractual continuity commitments verified during vendor due diligence and annual review.

11. Compliance

This policy is mandatory. Non-compliance shall be reported to the Business Continuity Manager and CISO and may trigger corrective action.

12. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually and after any major incident or material change.

13. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.29, A.5.30, A.8.13, A.8.14; SOC 2 A1.1–A1.3, CC7.5; and ASAE 3150 control objectives for availability and recovery.

Policy.description: Governs how changes to systems, applications, infrastructure and processes are authorised, tested and deployed.
Policy.title: Change Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Change Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Change Manager  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy ensures that all changes to information systems, applications, infrastructure, security configurations and supporting processes are authorised, risk-assessed, tested, documented and reviewable. It supports SOC 2 CC8.1, ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.8.32 and ASAE 3150 control objectives.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all changes affecting production systems, customer-facing services, security controls, data, identity systems and any system within the CDR data environment. It covers application code, infrastructure-as-code, configuration, schema, network, identity and supplier-managed systems.

3. Change Categories

CategoryDescriptionApproval
StandardPre-approved, low-risk, repeatable changes (e.g. routine patching)Pre-approved category owner
NormalNon-routine changes requiring assessmentChange Advisory Board (CAB)
EmergencyRequired to restore service or address active threatEmergency CAB; post-implementation review mandatory

4. Change Lifecycle

4.1 Request and Risk Assessment

Changes shall be raised in the change management system with: description, rationale, scope, risk assessment, security impact, rollback plan, test evidence and verification plan.

4.2 Approval

Approvals shall be recorded electronically. Production changes require approval by the change owner and (for Normal changes) the CAB. Security-impacting changes require CISO or delegate approval.

4.3 Testing

  • Functional and regression testing for application changes;
  • Security testing (SAST/DAST/SCA) per Secure SDLC Policy;
  • Performance and capacity testing for high-load changes;
  • Test evidence shall be attached to the change record.

4.4 Implementation

  • Implementation shall follow the documented plan and maintenance window;
  • Segregation of duties: developers shall not unilaterally deploy to production; deployments shall be performed via the CI/CD pipeline with approvals enforced.

4.5 Verification and Closure

Post-deployment verification, monitoring window observation, and closure with outcome (success/rollback/partial) shall be recorded.

5. Emergency Changes

Emergency changes may be implemented with verbal approval from the on-call Incident Commander and CISO/delegate, but shall be documented in the change system within 24 hours and undergo post-implementation review.

6. Segregation of Duties

  • Developers cannot self-approve their own production changes;
  • Production deployment credentials shall not be shared with developers;
  • Privileged production access for change activity shall be just-in-time and audited.

7. Logging and Audit

All change activity, approvals and deployment events shall be logged centrally and retained for at least 12 months. Logs shall be tamper-resistant and reviewed during access reviews and audits.

8. Configuration Management

Production configuration shall be managed as code where feasible, version-controlled, and synchronised between environments. Configuration drift shall be detected and remediated.

9. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Change ManagerOwns process; chairs CAB
CABReviews and approves Normal changes
Change InitiatorSubmits, plans, and verifies the change
CISOApproves security-impacting changes
Service OwnersAccept production deployment for owned services

10. Compliance

Unauthorised changes are prohibited. Detected unauthorised changes shall be treated as a security incident under the Incident Response Policy.

11. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually.

12. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.8.32 (and related A.8.27–A.8.31); SOC 2 CC8.1; and ASAE 3150 control objectives for change management.

Policy.description: Establishes due diligence, contracting and monitoring requirements for vendors and third parties.
Policy.title: Vendor and Third-Party Risk Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Vendor and Third-Party Risk Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Vendor Manager  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the requirements for the identification, assessment, contracting, onboarding, monitoring and offboarding of vendors and other third parties. It manages the risks introduced by suppliers to the confidentiality, integrity and availability of organisation and customer data, and supports ISO/IEC 27001, SOC 2, ASAE 3150 (subservice organisations) and CDR Rules supplier governance requirements.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all third parties (vendors, suppliers, contractors, subprocessors, outsourced service providers and subservice organisations) that process organisation data, integrate with organisation systems, or deliver services material to the organisation's operations — including those within the CDR data environment.

3. Vendor Tiering

TierCriteriaRequired Assurance
Tier 1 – CriticalHosts/processes Restricted data (incl. CDR/PII), critical operational dependency, or subservice organisationSOC 2 Type II / ISO 27001 cert; signed DPA; annual review; incident notification SLA
Tier 2 – HighProcesses Confidential data or material business dependencySecurity questionnaire; contract security clauses; annual review
Tier 3 – ModerateLimited access to Internal data onlyBasic due diligence; standard terms
Tier 4 – LowNo data access; non-materialStandard procurement only

4. Pre-Engagement Due Diligence

  • Business case and risk assessment by requesting team;
  • Vendor questionnaire scaled to tier (security, privacy, financial, ESG, sanctions);
  • Review of independent assurance (SOC 2/ISO/PCI/HIPAA) where applicable;
  • Verification of insurance, regulatory standing and incident history;
  • Approval by Vendor Manager and (Tier 1/2) CISO and Privacy Officer.

5. Contracting Requirements

  • Mandatory clauses: confidentiality, security obligations, audit/inspection rights, sub-processor approval, breach notification within agreed SLA, data return/deletion on termination, liability and indemnity;
  • Data Processing Agreement (DPA) for any vendor processing personal information;
  • Standard Contractual Clauses (or other lawful mechanism) for international transfers;
  • For CDR-relevant vendors: requirements aligned to CDR Privacy Safeguards 6, 11, 12 and CDR Rules.

6. Onboarding

  • Provisioning per least privilege; MFA-enforced;
  • Integration security review (IP allow-listing, mTLS, scoped tokens, scope of access logged);
  • Vendor record created in CMDB with owner, criticality and applicable frameworks.

7. Ongoing Monitoring

  • Annual reassessment for Tier 1/2 vendors with refreshed SOC/ISO reports and questionnaires;
  • Continuous monitoring of incident notifications and threat intelligence;
  • Service performance and SLA reviews at agreed cadence;
  • Subservice organisations under ASAE 3150 / SOC 2 documented with carve-out method or inclusive evidence and CUEC schedule.

8. Subservice Organisations and Subprocessors

Subservice organisations relied upon for the assurance scope shall be identified, documented and disclosed to assurance practitioners and customers as required. Engagement of new subprocessors handling personal information shall be notified to customers per contract.

9. Offboarding

  • Documented offboarding checklist covering access revocation, data return/deletion (with attestation), key rotation and contract closure;
  • Verification of deletion of organisation data within agreed timeframe.

10. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Vendor ManagerOwns vendor lifecycle and tier register
CISOApproves Tier 1/2 vendors; reviews assurance reports
Privacy Officer / DPOApproves DPAs and privacy aspects
Legal / ProcurementNegotiates contracts
Service OwnerDay-to-day relationship and operational oversight

11. Compliance

Engaging vendors outside this policy is prohibited and shall be treated as a control deficiency. Material vendor incidents shall be escalated under the Incident Response Policy.

12. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually.

13. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.5.19–A.5.23, A.5.34; SOC 2 CC9.2; ASAE 3150 subservice organisation requirements; ISO/IEC 27017 and 27018 cloud and personal data principles; and CDR Rules supplier provisions.

Policy.description: Defines security requirements through the employment lifecycle.
Policy.title: Human Resources Security Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Human Resources Security Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: HR  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines the information security requirements that apply throughout the employment lifecycle – prior to, during, and on change or termination of employment – to reduce the risk of human-related security breaches and to support compliance with ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.6, SOC 2 CC1.4 / CC1.5 and CDR fit-and-proper obligations.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all employees, contractors, consultants, interns, temporary workers and any other personnel performing work for the organisation, in any location, including those with access to the CDR data environment.

3. Pre-Employment

3.1 Background Screening

Background checks shall be performed prior to commencement, proportionate to the role and access required.
