For roles with access to Restricted data (including CDR data), background checks shall include identity verification, right-to-work, criminal history, and where appropriate financial probity and references. CDR-relevant associated persons shall meet the CDR fit-and-proper requirements (CDR Rules s 5.12).

3.2 Contractual Obligations

Employment and contractor agreements shall include confidentiality, intellectual property, acceptable use, code of conduct, conflict of interest, and ongoing post-termination obligations. Specific clauses shall apply to handling of personal and CDR data.

4. During Employment

4.1 Security Awareness and Training

  • Mandatory security awareness training within 30 days of commencement and annually thereafter;
  • Targeted role-based training (e.g. secure development, privileged access, CDR data handling);
  • Quarterly phishing simulations with remedial training for repeat clickers;
  • Completion rate target ≥ 95% – reported to management.

4.2 Performance and Conduct

Information security responsibilities shall be reflected in role descriptions and performance feedback where relevant. Breaches of security policy shall be addressed through the disciplinary process.

4.3 Role Changes

On change of role, access shall be re-evaluated using the joiner-mover-leaver (JML) process; access no longer required shall be removed within 5 business days.

5. Termination and Change of Engagement

  • Same-day revocation of access on termination (or sooner if security risk);
  • Return of all organisation assets (laptops, mobile devices, tokens, badges, documents);
  • Exit interview to remind of ongoing obligations and reporting channels;
  • For privileged roles, additional review of recent activity logs and credential rotation.

6. Disciplinary Process

A formal disciplinary process shall apply to personnel who breach this policy or related policies. The process shall be fair, documented, proportionate and consider the severity of the breach.

7. Contractors and Third-Party Personnel

Contractors shall be subject to equivalent screening, training and access governance as employees, proportionate to the role. Third-party personnel records shall be managed by the engaging service owner with HR oversight.

8. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
HROwns this policy; manages screening and disciplinary process
CISODefines training and access requirements
Hiring ManagersConfirm role security requirements; approve access
ITProvisioning and revocation per JML process
All PersonnelComply with this policy and supporting policies

9. Compliance

Non-compliance may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination. Significant breaches shall be reported to the CISO and may be treated as a security incident.

10. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually and on material legislative or organisational change.

11. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.6.1–A.6.6; SOC 2 CC1.4, CC1.5, CC2.2; and CDR fit-and-proper requirements (CDR Rules s 5.12).

Policy.description: Defines approved cryptographic algorithms, key management practices and TLS standards.
Policy.title: Cryptography and Key Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Cryptography and Key Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Security Architecture  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the approved cryptographic algorithms, protocols and key management practices used to protect the confidentiality, integrity and authenticity of information. It supports ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.8.24, SOC 2 CC6.1 and CC6.7, ISO/IEC 27018, and CDR Privacy Safeguard 12 (Schedule 2 Part 1).

2. Scope

This policy applies to all cryptographic use within the organisation, including cryptography in transit, at rest, in use, in code, in identity systems, and in third-party services that protect organisation or CDR data.

3. Approved Algorithms and Protocols

PurposeApprovedProhibited
Symmetric encryptionAES-256 (GCM preferred)DES, 3DES, RC4
Asymmetric encryptionRSA ≥ 2048 bit; ECC P-256 / P-384RSA < 2048; DSA < 2048
HashingSHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512MD5, SHA-1
Password hashingArgon2id, bcrypt, PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256Plain hashing without salt
TransportTLS 1.2 (with secure ciphers) minimum; TLS 1.3 preferredSSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1
Email transportTLS, MTA-STS, DMARC, DKIMCleartext SMTP for sensitive data

4. Use Requirements

  • All Confidential and Restricted data shall be encrypted at rest using approved algorithms;
  • All data in transit across untrusted networks shall be encrypted using approved protocols;
  • CDR data, personal information and authentication secrets shall always be encrypted at rest with managed keys;
  • Hard-coded keys, secrets or credentials in source code, configuration or images are prohibited.

5. Key Management

5.1 Generation

Keys shall be generated using approved cryptographically secure random number generators. Where supported, generation shall occur within an HSM or managed KMS.

5.2 Storage

Keys shall be stored in the centralised Key Management Service (KMS) or HSM. Application code shall reference keys by ID and never persist key material outside the KMS/HSM.

5.3 Distribution and Access

Access to keys shall be governed by least-privilege IAM, MFA-enforced, logged and reviewed at least quarterly. Separation of duties shall apply between key custodians and users.

5.4 Rotation

  • Data encryption keys: rotated at least annually and immediately on suspected compromise;
  • TLS certificates: lifetimes shall comply with public CA limits (≤ 13 months for public, internal per architecture standards);
  • API keys, tokens and service credentials: rotated at least annually and on personnel changes;
  • Root keys: rotated per HSM/KMS vendor recommendation.

5.5 Backup and Recovery

Key material shall be backed up using HSM/KMS-supported export controls under multi-party authorisation, stored in geographically separated secure locations, and tested for recovery at least annually.

5.6 Destruction

Decommissioned keys shall be destroyed using HSM/KMS-supported destroy operations with confirmation of zeroisation and audit log evidence.

6. Code, Secrets and Pipelines

  • Secrets shall be stored in approved secret management tooling (e.g. HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager);
  • CI/CD pipelines shall retrieve secrets at runtime; never embed in repos or images;
  • Pre-commit and SAST/secret-scan tooling shall block secrets from entering source control.

7. Customer-Managed and Third-Party Keys

Where customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) or BYOK arrangements are offered, the responsibilities and operational procedures shall be documented in the customer agreement and runbooks. Third-party providers handling keys shall hold appropriate certifications.

8. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Security ArchitectureOwns standards and approved algorithms
CISOApproves exceptions; reviews compliance
Key CustodiansOperate KMS/HSM under SoD
EngineeringImplement cryptography per this policy

9. Exceptions

Exceptions require written CISO approval, documented compensating controls, and a remediation plan with target date.

10. Compliance

Use of unapproved cryptography or insecure key handling shall be treated as a control failure and may be a security incident.

11. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually and as cryptographic standards evolve.

12. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.8.24; SOC 2 CC6.1, CC6.7, C1.1; ISO/IEC 27018; and CDR Privacy Safeguard 12 (Schedule 2 Part 1).

Policy.description: Defines secure SDLC requirements, code review, testing and dependency management.
Policy.title: Secure Software Development Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Secure Software Development Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Engineering / AppSec  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines the secure software development lifecycle (SDLC) requirements that ensure security is designed, built, tested and maintained in all software developed or materially configured by the organisation. It supports ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.8.25–A.8.34 and SOC 2 CC7.1, CC8.1.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all in-house developed software, infrastructure-as-code, configuration-as-code, integrations and customisations of third-party platforms that support the organisation's services or interact with Confidential or Restricted data, including the CDR data environment.

3. SDLC Requirements

3.1 Requirements and Design

  • Security requirements shall be elicited during requirements definition;
  • Threat modelling (e.g. STRIDE) shall be performed for new services and material design changes, with documented mitigations;
  • Privacy-by-design and data minimisation principles shall be applied for any feature processing personal or CDR data.

3.2 Coding Standards

  • Engineers shall follow approved secure coding standards aligned with OWASP Top 10, OWASP ASVS, and language-specific guidance;
  • Input validation, output encoding, parameterised queries, and safe deserialisation are mandatory;
  • Secrets and credentials shall not be embedded in code (see Cryptography Policy).

3.3 Code Review

  • All production-bound changes shall undergo peer review by an engineer other than the author;
  • Reviews shall consider security, correctness, performance, maintainability and test coverage;
  • Security-sensitive changes shall require AppSec or Security Architect review.

3.4 Automated Security Testing

StageToolingRequired
Pre-commitSecret scanning, lintersYes
CISAST, SCA (dependency vulnerability scan)Yes
Pre-deployContainer/IaC scanningYes
Post-deployDAST against staging or pre-prodTier 1 services

3.5 Vulnerability Management in SDLC

  • Critical and High findings shall block the merge or release;
  • Medium findings shall be tracked with risk-based remediation timeline;
  • Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) shall be generated for releases of Tier 1 services.

3.6 Build, Release and Deployment

  • Builds shall occur in trusted, ephemeral CI environments;
  • Artefacts shall be signed and stored in trusted registries;
  • Production deployment shall be gated by automated checks, approvals and the Change Management Policy;
  • Segregation of duties shall be enforced (developers cannot self-approve production deploys).

3.7 Operational Security

  • Logging and monitoring per the Logging and Monitoring Policy;
  • Feature flags and progressive rollout for risk reduction;
  • Defined rollback strategy for every release.

4. Penetration Testing

Independent penetration testing shall be performed at least annually for production-facing applications and APIs (and after material change), with findings tracked through to remediation. CDR-bounded services shall be included in scope.

5. Training

Engineers shall complete secure development training annually, with role-specific content (front-end, back-end, mobile, infrastructure-as-code, AI). Champions shall be identified per team.

6. Open Source and Third-Party Components

  • All third-party components shall be approved and tracked in the SBOM;
  • Vulnerable components shall be remediated per the vulnerability management SLA;
  • Licences shall be reviewed for compatibility.

7. AI and Generative Code

Use of AI coding assistants is permitted under approved tooling only. Generated code remains the responsibility of the engineer and shall be reviewed under the same standards as human-written code. Customer or CDR data shall not be sent to third-party AI services without an approved exception.

8. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
CTO / VP EngineeringResourcing and accountability for secure SDLC
AppSec / Security ArchitectureStandards, tooling, threat modelling, exceptions
Engineering ManagersEnforce SDLC compliance in their teams
EngineersApply secure coding and testing
CISOApproves material risk exceptions

9. Compliance

Non-compliance shall result in build/release blockers and may trigger corrective action under the disciplinary process.

10. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually.

11. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.8.25–A.8.34; SOC 2 CC7.1, CC8.1; OWASP ASVS; and supports CDR Privacy Safeguard 12 obligations applicable to the CDR data environment.

Policy.description: Public-facing CDR Policy required by Privacy Safeguard 1 describing collection, use, disclosure and management of CDR data.
Policy.title: CDR Policy
Policy.policy_content:
CDR Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Data Protection Officer  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. About This Policy

This Consumer Data Right (CDR) Policy is published to satisfy Privacy Safeguard 1 (open and transparent management of CDR data) under Schedule 2 of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) and the Competition and Consumer (Consumer Data Right) Rules 2020 (CDR Rules). It explains how the organisation manages CDR data, the rights of CDR consumers, and how complaints can be raised.

2. Definitions

  • CDR data means data within the meaning of section 56AI of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) and includes any information derived from CDR data;
  • CDR consumer means an individual or entity to whom CDR data relates and who may share that data using the CDR;
  • Accredited Data Recipient (ADR) means a person accredited by the ACCC under the CDR Rules;
  • Designated Sectors include banking, energy and (over time) other sectors as designated by the Treasurer.

3. Categories of CDR Data Collected and Held

The categories of CDR data that may be collected and held depend on the consumer's authorisation and may include account data, transaction data, customer data and product data permitted under the relevant designation instrument.

4. Purposes of Collection and Use

CDR data is collected and used only:

  • For the specific purposes consented to by the CDR consumer;
  • For directly related secondary purposes permitted under the CDR Rules;
  • To meet legal obligations;
  • To prevent or investigate fraud or unlawful activity, where permitted.

5. How Consent Is Obtained, Managed and Withdrawn

  • Consent is express, voluntary, time-limited and specific, in line with the CDR Rules and the Data Standards (CX Standards);
  • Consumers are presented with a consent dashboard to view, amend or withdraw consents at any time;
  • On withdrawal, the organisation ceases collection and uses the CDR data only as permitted by the CDR Rules, and de-identifies or deletes data in accordance with Privacy Safeguard 12 and the Retention Schedule.

6. Disclosure of CDR Data

CDR data is disclosed only:

  • To Accredited Data Recipients or persons authorised by the consumer;
  • As required or authorised by law;
  • In accordance with the consumer's express consent and the CDR Rules.

The organisation does not disclose CDR data overseas other than as permitted by the consumer's consent and the CDR Rules.

7. Storage, Security and Retention

  • CDR data is held within the organisation's defined CDR data environment subject to the controls required by Privacy Safeguard 12 and CDR Rules Schedule 2;
  • CDR data is encrypted at rest and in transit, access is MFA-enforced and least-privilege, and activity is logged and monitored;
  • Retention is limited to the period for which consent was given or as required by law; on expiry data is deleted or de-identified.

8. Consumer Rights

RightHow to Exercise
Access CDR data we hold (PS 11/12)Through the consent dashboard or by contacting the DPO
Correction (PS 13)Submit a request to the DPO; the organisation will respond as soon as practicable
Withdraw consentThrough the consent dashboard at any time
Request deletion / de-identificationThrough the consent dashboard or DPO

9. Complaints and Dispute Resolution

CDR-related complaints can be lodged with the organisation's Data Protection Officer. The organisation operates an Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) process consistent with ASIC RG 271 and CDR Rules timeframes. Unresolved complaints can be escalated to the Australian Financial Complaints Authority (AFCA) as the approved External Dispute Resolution (EDR) scheme. Privacy complaints can also be made to the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC).

10. Eligible Data Breach Notification

If an eligible data breach involving CDR data occurs, the organisation will notify the ACCC, OAIC and affected CDR consumers in accordance with section 56 of the CDR Rules and the Notifiable Data Breaches scheme under the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth).

11. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
CEOExecutive accountability for CDR compliance
DPOOwns this policy; consumer rights handling
CISOPrivacy Safeguard 12 (information security)
Compliance ManagerAccreditation and ACCC reporting
LegalInterpretation of CDR Rules and contracts

12. Contacting Us

For questions about this Policy or to exercise your rights, contact the Data Protection Officer using the contact details on the organisation's public website. Updates to this policy will be published on the same website with an effective date.

13. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually and on material change to the CDR Rules, designations or organisational arrangements.

14. Related Obligations

This policy supports compliance with CDR Privacy Safeguards 1, 3, 6, 11, 12 and 13 (Schedule 2 of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth)), the CDR Rules (including section 56), and the Privacy Act 1988 (Cth).

Policy.description: Establishes log generation, retention, protection and monitoring requirements.
Policy.title: Logging and Monitoring Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Logging and Monitoring Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Security Operations  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the requirements for the generation, collection, retention, protection, monitoring and review of security-relevant log data. Effective logging and monitoring underpin detection, response and forensic investigation, and support ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.8.15–A.8.17, SOC 2 CC7.2–CC7.4 and CDR Privacy Safeguard 12.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all systems, applications, infrastructure, network devices, identity systems, security tooling and third-party services that support the organisation's operations or process Confidential or Restricted data – including the CDR data environment.

3. Log Generation Requirements

CategoryRequired Events
AuthenticationSuccessful and failed sign-ins, MFA challenges, account lockouts, session creation/termination
AuthorisationPrivilege elevation, role/permission changes, denied access
AdministrativeConfiguration changes, account creation/modification/deletion, key/secret operations
Data AccessRead/write/delete of Restricted data (incl. CDR/PII), bulk export, schema changes
ApplicationCritical errors, security exceptions, integration failures, anomalous business events
Network / InfrastructureFirewall denies, IDS/IPS alerts, VPN/ZTNA sessions, DNS anomalies
Security ToolingEDR detections, SIEM rule fires, DLP events, vulnerability scan results

4. Log Quality and Time

  • Logs shall include accurate, synchronised UTC timestamps (NTP);
  • Each event shall include sufficient context (actor, action, target, source, outcome);
  • Sensitive payloads (passwords, secrets, full PII/CDR data) shall not be logged.

5. Centralisation and Protection

  • Logs shall be forwarded to the centralised SIEM in near-real-time;
  • Log storage shall be append-only, access-controlled and tamper-evident;
  • Access to log management shall be MFA-enforced, least-privilege, and reviewed quarterly;
  • Modifications to log configuration shall themselves be logged.

6. Retention

Log TypeOnline HotCold / Archive
Authentication / Authorisation12 months+ 12 months
Administrative / Privileged12 months+ 24 months
Data Access (CDR/PII)12 months+ 24 months (or longer per regulator)
Network / Infrastructure6 months+ 6 months

7. Monitoring and Detection

  • The SIEM shall implement use cases mapped to MITRE ATT&CK and aligned to organisational threat model;
  • 24x7 monitoring shall be in place for High and Critical alerts (in-house or via MDR);
  • Alert tuning shall be performed continuously to maintain quality and reduce noise;
  • Detection coverage gaps shall be tracked as control deficiencies.

8. Review and Reporting

  • High-risk events (privileged access, off-hours admin, mass data access) shall be reviewed at least weekly;
  • Logging coverage health (sources reporting, ingestion volume, gaps) shall be reviewed monthly;
  • Logging and monitoring metrics shall be reported to the Security Steering Committee quarterly.

9. Privacy and Legal Considerations

Logging shall be designed to meet privacy principles – collected for a defined purpose, minimal data, retained only as long as necessary. Personal information in logs shall be pseudonymised where feasible. Workforce monitoring shall be in line with applicable law and the Acceptable Use Policy.

10. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Security OperationsOwns SIEM, detection content and 24x7 monitoring
CISOApproves logging standard and exceptions
Service OwnersOnboard owned systems to SIEM with required events
Engineering / ITImplement application and infrastructure logging
Privacy / DPOReview logging of personal information

11. Compliance

Disabling, tampering with, or bypassing logging is prohibited and shall be treated as a security incident. Gaps in coverage shall be remediated through the corrective action process.

12. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually and on material technology or threat-landscape change.

13. Related Controls

This policy supports ISO/IEC 27001:2022 Annex A.8.15, A.8.16, A.8.17; SOC 2 CC7.2–CC7.4; and CDR Privacy Safeguard 12 (Schedule 2 Part 1).

Information Security Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This Information Security Policy establishes the organisation's commitment to protecting the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of all information assets. It provides the overarching framework for all information security activities and is supported by a suite of topic-specific policies and procedures.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all employees, contractors, consultants, temporary workers, and any other persons who have access to the organisation's information assets, systems, and facilities, regardless of location. It covers all information assets including digital data, physical documents, software, hardware, networks, and cloud services.

3. Policy Statement

The organisation is committed to:

  • Protecting information from unauthorised access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction;
  • Ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information in accordance with business requirements and applicable legal and regulatory obligations;
  • Meeting applicable contractual, regulatory, and legal requirements related to information security;
  • Maintaining an effective Information Security Management System (ISMS) in alignment with ISO/IEC 27001;
  • Ensuring security controls are commensurate with the risks identified through formal risk assessment;
  • Promoting a culture of security awareness across the organisation.

4. Information Security Objectives

The organisation shall establish, maintain, and review information security objectives aligned with this policy. Objectives shall be measurable where practicable and communicated to relevant personnel. Current objectives include:

  • Achieve and maintain ISO 27001 certification;
  • Obtain a clean SOC 2 Type II assurance report;
  • Complete ASAE 3150 assurance engagement with no qualified opinion;
  • Achieve zero critical unpatched vulnerabilities within SLA timeframes;
  • Maintain security awareness training completion rate above 95%.

5. Roles and Responsibilities

RoleResponsibility
Board of DirectorsOversight and approval of information security strategy and risk appetite
CEOExecutive accountability for information security; approval of this policy
CISOOwnership, maintenance, and implementation of the ISMS; reporting to executive on security posture
All StaffCompliance with this policy and all supporting policies; reporting of security incidents
IT / DevOpsImplementation and maintenance of technical security controls
HRIntegration of security requirements into employment lifecycle

6. Risk Management

The organisation shall conduct formal information security risk assessments at least annually and whenever significant changes occur. Risks shall be assessed using the organisation's risk assessment methodology and treated in accordance with the risk treatment plan. Risk acceptance decisions shall be documented and approved by appropriate management.

7. Compliance

All personnel must comply with this policy and all supporting information security policies and procedures. Non-compliance may result in disciplinary action, up to and including termination of employment or contract. Suspected non-compliance shall be reported to the CISO.

8. Policy Review

This policy shall be reviewed at least annually by the CISO and approved by the CEO. It shall also be reviewed following any significant incident, material change to the organisation, or change in applicable legal or regulatory requirements.

9. Related Documents

  • Access Control Policy
  • Data Classification and Handling Policy
  • Incident Response Plan
  • Business Continuity Plan
  • Risk Management Policy
  • Acceptable Use Policy
  • Change Management Policy
  • Vendor Management Policy
  • HR Security Policy
  • Physical Security Policy
  • Cryptography Policy
Policy.description: Overarching policy defining the organisation's approach to information security management.
Policy.title: Information Security Policy

Policy.policy_content:
Access Control Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes requirements for controlling access to the organisation's information systems, applications, and data. It ensures that access is granted on a need-to-know, least-privilege basis and that access rights are managed throughout the user lifecycle.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all information systems, applications, databases, cloud services, and network infrastructure owned or operated by the organisation. It applies to all users including employees, contractors, service accounts, and privileged users.

3. Access Control Principles

  • Least Privilege: Users shall be granted the minimum level of access required to perform their duties.
  • Need to Know: Access to sensitive information shall be restricted to those with a legitimate business requirement.
  • Separation of Duties: Conflicting roles and responsibilities shall be separated to prevent fraud and error.
  • Default Deny: Access shall be denied unless explicitly granted.

4. User Access Management

4.1 User Registration

All access requests must be submitted via the formal access request process and approved by the relevant data owner or manager before access is provisioned. User accounts shall be uniquely identified and must not be shared.

4.2 Privileged Access

Privileged access (administrative accounts) shall be strictly controlled. Privileged accounts shall only be used for activities requiring elevated permissions. All privileged access shall be managed via the organisation's Privileged Access Management (PAM) solution with session recording enabled.

4.3 Access Reviews

All user access rights shall be reviewed at least quarterly by the relevant system owner or manager. Access that is no longer required shall be removed within five (5) business days of the review decision.

4.4 Access Revocation

Access to all systems shall be revoked on or before the last day of employment or contract. The HR and IT teams shall follow the offboarding checklist to ensure timely revocation across all systems.

5. Authentication Requirements

5.1 Multi-Factor Authentication

Multi-factor authentication (MFA) is mandatory for:

  • All remote access to internal systems;
  • All access to production environments and administrative consoles;
  • All access to cloud infrastructure and SaaS applications containing sensitive data;
  • All privileged user accounts.

5.2 Password Requirements

Where passwords are used, they shall meet the following minimum requirements:

  • Minimum length of 12 characters;
  • Complexity requirements including a mix of character types;
  • Passwords shall not be reused for at least 12 generations;
  • Accounts shall be locked after 10 consecutive failed login attempts.

6. Remote Access

Remote access to internal systems shall be conducted via approved VPN or Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) solution. MFA is required for all remote access sessions. Remote access shall be logged and monitored.

7. Service Accounts

Service accounts shall be documented, have strong randomly generated passwords or use certificate-based authentication, and shall be reviewed annually. Service accounts shall be granted only the permissions required for the specific function.

8. Third-Party Access

Third-party vendors requiring access to systems shall be granted time-limited access only. All third-party access shall be approved by the relevant data owner and monitored for the duration of the access period.

9. Related Controls

This policy supports SOC 2 criteria CC6.1–CC6.5 and ISO 27001 controls A.8.2, A.8.3, A.8.5.
Policy.description: Defines requirements for user access management, authentication, and authorisation across all systems.
Policy.title: Access Control Policy

Policy.description: Establishes how the organisation classifies, labels, and handles information assets based on sensitivity.
Policy.title: Data Classification and Handling Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Data Classification and Handling Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines how the organisation classifies its information assets and establishes handling requirements appropriate to each classification level. It ensures that sensitive information receives proportionate protection and that all personnel understand their obligations.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all information created, received, stored, processed, or transmitted by the organisation, regardless of format (digital or physical) or location.

3. Classification Levels

ClassificationDescriptionExamples
PublicInformation approved for unrestricted public disclosureMarketing materials, public website content
InternalInformation for internal use only; not for external parties without authorisationInternal procedures, staff communications
ConfidentialSensitive business information; limited distribution on need-to-know basisFinancial data, contracts, customer data, employee records
RestrictedHighly sensitive information; most stringent controls applyAuthentication credentials, cryptographic keys, audit findings, personal health information

4. Handling Requirements

4.1 Storage

  • Public: No special requirements.
  • Internal: Store on authorised systems; access controlled by role.
  • Confidential: Encrypted at rest; access logging enabled.
  • Restricted: Encrypted at rest with AES-256 or equivalent; strict access controls; access reviewed quarterly.

4.2 Transmission

  • Confidential and Restricted data must be encrypted in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher.
  • Restricted data must not be transmitted via personal email or unapproved file-sharing services.

4.3 Retention and Disposal

Data shall be retained in accordance with the Data Retention Schedule. When no longer required, data shall be securely deleted (digital) or shredded (physical). Disposal shall be documented.

5. Labelling

All documents classified as Confidential or Restricted shall be clearly labelled with the classification level in the document header and footer. Electronic files shall be named or tagged to reflect their classification where practicable.

6. Responsibilities

Data owners are responsible for classifying information under their stewardship. All staff are responsible for handling information according to its classification level.

7. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.5.1, SOC 2 CC2.1, CC6.7.

Incident Response Plan

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This Incident Response Plan (IRP) defines the organisation's approach to identifying, containing, eradicating, and recovering from information security incidents. It ensures a consistent, effective, and timely response that minimises impact to business operations and meets notification obligations.

2. Scope

This plan applies to all information security incidents affecting the organisation's systems, data, personnel, or reputation. It covers incidents involving but not limited to: data breaches, ransomware, unauthorised access, denial of service, insider threats, and physical security events.

3. Incident Severity Classification

SeverityDescriptionResponse Time
P1 – CriticalActive breach, ransomware, confirmed data loss affecting customers or critical systems downImmediate (within 1 hour)
P2 – HighSuspected breach, significant system compromise, or material service degradationWithin 4 hours
P3 – MediumIsolated security event with limited impact; policy violationWithin 1 business day
P4 – LowMinor event with no immediate impact; informationalWithin 3 business days

4. Incident Response Phases

4.1 Preparation

Maintain and test this plan annually. Ensure Incident Response Team (IRT) members are trained. Maintain contact lists, forensic tools, and communication templates ready for activation.

4.2 Identification and Detection

Incidents may be detected via: SIEM alerts, user reports, third-party notifications, or external intelligence. All potential incidents must be reported to the CISO immediately via the security incident hotline or ticketing system.

4.3 Containment

Upon confirmation of an incident, the IRT shall implement short-term containment measures to prevent further damage. This may include isolating affected systems, revoking compromised credentials, blocking malicious IP addresses, or disabling affected accounts.

4.4 Eradication

The root cause of the incident shall be identified and removed. This may include removing malware, applying patches, closing attack vectors, and resetting compromised credentials.

4.5 Recovery

Affected systems shall be restored to normal operation using clean backups or rebuilt environments. Systems shall be monitored closely following restoration to confirm normal operation and detect recurrence.

4.6 Post-Incident Review

A post-incident review (PIR) shall be conducted within 10 business days for all P1 and P2 incidents. The PIR shall document the timeline, impact, root cause, response effectiveness, and lessons learned. Findings shall be actioned and tracked to completion.

5. Notification and Communication

5.1 Internal Communication

The CISO shall notify executive leadership of all P1 and P2 incidents within two (2) hours of confirmation. Regular status updates shall be provided throughout the incident.

5.2 Regulatory and Customer Notification

Where a notifiable data breach has occurred under applicable privacy legislation (e.g., Australian Privacy Act – Notifiable Data Breaches scheme), notification shall be made to the relevant regulator within 72 hours and to affected individuals without undue delay. Legal counsel shall be engaged prior to external notifications.

6. Incident Response Team

RoleResponsibility
Incident CommanderCISO – overall incident coordination and decision-making
Technical LeadIT/Security Engineer – technical investigation and remediation
Communications LeadCEO / Legal – internal and external communications
HR RepresentativeHR Manager – employee-related matters

7. Evidence Preservation

All incident evidence shall be preserved in accordance with chain-of-custody procedures. Logs, system images, and artefacts shall not be deleted until the investigation is complete and approved by the CISO.

8. Related Controls

Supports SOC 2 CC7.3, CC7.4, CC7.5 and ISO 27001 A.5.24–A.5.28.
Policy.description: Procedures for detecting, classifying, responding to, and recovering from information security incidents.
Policy.title: Incident Response Plan

Policy.description: Ensures the organisation can maintain critical functions and recover systems following a disruptive event.
Policy.title: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CTO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the framework for business continuity planning (BCP) and disaster recovery (DR) to ensure that the organisation can continue critical operations and recover IT systems following a disruptive event.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all critical business functions, information systems, and supporting infrastructure. It encompasses scenarios including but not limited to: natural disasters, cyberattacks, infrastructure failures, pandemic, and supplier disruption.

3. Business Impact Analysis

A Business Impact Analysis (BIA) shall be conducted at least annually to identify critical business functions, their dependencies, and the impact of disruption over time. The BIA shall define Recovery Time Objectives (RTOs) and Recovery Point Objectives (RPOs) for all critical systems.

System TierDescriptionRTORPO
Tier 1 – Mission CriticalSystems that, if unavailable, immediately halt business operations4 hours1 hour
Tier 2 – Business CriticalSystems important to operations but with some manual workarounds24 hours4 hours
Tier 3 – Business ImportantSystems supporting operational efficiency but not immediately critical72 hours24 hours

4. Backup Requirements

  • All Tier 1 and Tier 2 systems must have automated daily backups;
  • Backups must be stored in a geographically separate location or cloud region;
  • Backup integrity must be tested monthly via restore verification;
  • Immutable (WORM) backups must be implemented for critical data to protect against ransomware;
  • Backup access shall be restricted and monitored.

5. Disaster Recovery

A Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) shall be maintained for all Tier 1 systems documenting step-by-step recovery procedures. The DRP shall be tested at least annually via a full failover exercise and the results documented. Deficiencies shall be remediated within 90 days.

6. Business Continuity Plan

The Business Continuity Plan shall document procedures for maintaining critical business functions during a disruption, including manual workarounds, alternative work locations, communication procedures, and escalation chains. The BCP shall be reviewed annually and following any significant incident.

7. Testing and Maintenance

BCP and DRP exercises shall be conducted at least annually. Tests may include tabletop exercises, functional tests, or full failover simulations. Test results shall be reported to executive leadership and the board.

8. Related Controls

Supports SOC 2 A1.2, A1.3 and ISO 27001 A.5.29, A.5.30.

Policy.description: Controls for managing changes to IT systems, applications, and infrastructure to prevent unauthorised or untested changes.
Policy.title: Change Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Change Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CTO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes controls for managing changes to the organisation's information systems, applications, and infrastructure. It ensures that all changes are authorised, tested, and documented to minimise the risk of service disruption, security vulnerabilities, or non-compliance.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all changes to production systems, applications, infrastructure, network components, databases, and cloud services. It applies to all personnel involved in the delivery, approval, or implementation of changes.

3. Change Types

Change TypeDescriptionApproval Required
StandardPre-approved, low-risk, well-understood changes with defined proceduresPre-approved; log only
NormalPlanned changes requiring risk assessment and approval before implementationChange Advisory Board (CAB)
EmergencyUrgent changes required to restore service or remediate a critical security vulnerabilityCISO or CTO + retrospective CAB review

4. Change Process

4.1 Request

All changes must be submitted via the change management system with a description, business justification, risk assessment, test plan, rollback plan, and implementation timeline.

4.2 Assessment and Approval

Normal changes must be reviewed by the Change Advisory Board (CAB) and approved before implementation. The CAB shall meet at least weekly. Changes with significant security implications must be approved by the CISO.

4.3 Testing

All changes must be tested in a non-production environment before deployment to production. Security testing (including vulnerability scanning) must be performed for changes affecting security-relevant components.

4.4 Implementation

Changes shall be implemented during approved maintenance windows unless classified as emergency. All implementation steps shall be documented. Changes must include a verified rollback plan.

4.5 Post-Implementation Review

All normal and emergency changes shall be reviewed within five (5) business days of implementation to confirm success and identify any issues.

5. Emergency Changes

Emergency changes may bypass the standard approval process to address critical incidents. However, they must be approved by at least two authorised managers (CISO or CTO) and submitted for retrospective CAB review within two (2) business days.

6. Segregation of Duties

No individual shall both request and approve their own change. Development staff shall not deploy directly to production environments without an independent approval step.

7. Related Controls

Supports SOC 2 CC8.1, CC6.8 and ISO 27001 A.8.32.

Vendor and Third-Party Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Compliance Manager  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the requirements for managing information security risks associated with third-party vendors, suppliers, and subservice organisations that access, process, store, or transmit the organisation's information or systems.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all third-party relationships where the vendor has access to the organisation's information assets, systems, or premises. It applies to all vendor types including cloud service providers, IT service providers, professional services firms, and subservice organisations relied upon for SOC 2 / ASAE 3150 in-scope services.

3. Vendor Classification

TierDescriptionAssessment Frequency
Tier 1 – CriticalVendors with access to sensitive or personal data, or those providing critical servicesAnnual full assessment
Tier 2 – HighVendors with limited data access or providing important (non-critical) servicesAnnual questionnaire + review of certifications
Tier 3 – StandardVendors with no data access or providing commoditised servicesBiennial review; certification review only

4. Vendor Onboarding

Before engaging a Tier 1 or Tier 2 vendor, the following must be completed:

  • Completion of the organisation's vendor security questionnaire;
  • Review of the vendor's security certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 2, etc.);
  • Risk assessment by the CISO;
  • Execution of a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) where personal data is involved;
  • Inclusion of information security obligations in the contract;
  • Right-to-audit clause included for Tier 1 vendors.

5. Ongoing Monitoring

Vendor security posture shall be monitored on an ongoing basis. Tier 1 vendors shall be reviewed annually. The organisation shall request updated SOC 2 reports, ISO 27001 certificates, or equivalent attestations each year. Material changes to vendor security posture or ownership shall trigger an out-of-cycle review.

6. Subservice Organisations (ASAE 3150)

For the purposes of the ASAE 3150 engagement, subservice organisations that are relevant to the in-scope services shall be identified and disclosed in the system description. The organisation shall monitor the controls of subservice organisations by reviewing their SOC 2 or equivalent assurance reports annually and documenting complementary controls it has implemented.

7. Vendor Offboarding

Upon termination of a vendor relationship, all access to the organisation's systems and data shall be revoked immediately. The vendor shall certify destruction or return of all organisational data. Shared credentials shall be rotated.

8. Related Controls

Supports SOC 2 CC9.2 and ISO 27001 A.5.19–A.5.22.
Policy.description: Requirements for assessing, onboarding, monitoring, and offboarding third-party vendors with access to information or systems.
Policy.title: Vendor and Third-Party Management Policy

Policy.description: Defines acceptable use of the organisation's IT resources, systems, and data by all users.
Policy.title: Acceptable Use Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Acceptable Use Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines the acceptable use of the organisation's IT resources, systems, networks, and data. It protects the organisation's assets from misuse, legal liability, and security risks.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all employees, contractors, and third parties using the organisation's IT resources, including computers, mobile devices, email, internet, cloud services, and internal systems.

3. Acceptable Use

Users may use organisational IT resources for:

  • Performing their assigned job duties;
  • Reasonable incidental personal use that does not interfere with work performance or consume excessive resources;
  • Accessing systems and data for which they have been authorised.

4. Prohibited Activities

The following activities are strictly prohibited:

  • Accessing, downloading, storing, or distributing illegal, offensive, or discriminatory content;
  • Using organisational systems for commercial activities unrelated to work;
  • Attempting to circumvent security controls, access unauthorised systems, or exploit vulnerabilities;
  • Sharing login credentials or allowing others to use your account;
  • Installing unauthorised software on organisational devices;
  • Transmitting confidential data via unapproved channels (e.g., personal email, consumer file sharing);
  • Mining cryptocurrency or performing activities that consume excessive computational resources;
  • Using organisational resources to harass, defame, or discriminate against others.

5. Monitoring

The organisation reserves the right to monitor the use of its IT resources in accordance with applicable law and privacy regulations. Users have no expectation of privacy when using organisational systems. Monitoring may include logging of internet activity, email scanning, and endpoint monitoring.

6. Personal Devices (BYOD)

Employees using personal devices to access organisational systems must comply with the organisation's Mobile Device Policy, which includes enrolling the device in the Mobile Device Management (MDM) solution and maintaining up-to-date operating system and security patches.

7. Consequences of Non-Compliance

Violations of this policy may result in disciplinary action up to and including termination of employment or contract, and may be referred to law enforcement authorities where illegal activity is suspected.

8. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.5.10 and SOC 2 CC1.1.

HR Security Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: HR Manager  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines information security requirements applicable throughout the employment and contractor lifecycle to reduce the risk of human-related security incidents, including insider threats, data mishandling, and social engineering.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all permanent employees, temporary workers, contractors, and interns engaged by the organisation.

3. Pre-Employment Screening

Prior to commencing employment or a contractor engagement, the organisation shall conduct background verification checks appropriate to the role. For roles with access to sensitive data or systems, checks shall include:

  • Identity verification;
  • Employment history verification (past 5 years);
  • Criminal record check (where legally permissible);
  • Reference checks (minimum two).

All background checks shall be conducted by an approved third-party screening provider and results documented in the HR system.

4. Terms and Conditions of Employment

All employees and contractors shall sign an employment contract or engagement agreement prior to commencing work. Contracts shall include:

  • Obligations to comply with the organisation's information security policies;
  • Confidentiality obligations;
  • Obligations regarding intellectual property;
  • Acknowledgement that systems may be monitored.

5. Security Awareness and Training

All staff shall complete mandatory information security awareness training:

  • Within 30 days of commencement (onboarding training);
  • Annually thereafter (refresher training);
  • Role-specific training for high-risk roles (e.g., administrators, developers, finance staff).

Training completion shall be tracked and reported to the CISO quarterly. Staff who fail to complete training within the required timeframe shall have access suspended pending completion.

6. Disciplinary Process

Violations of information security policies shall be handled through the organisation's disciplinary process. Disciplinary action shall be proportionate to the severity of the breach. Serious breaches (e.g., deliberate data theft) may result in immediate termination and referral to law enforcement.

7. Offboarding

Upon termination or resignation, the following steps shall be completed on or before the final day:

  • All system access revoked;
  • Company devices returned and wiped;
  • Ongoing confidentiality obligations confirmed in writing;
  • Exit interview completed.

8. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.6.1–A.6.6 and SOC 2 CC1.4.
Policy.description: Defines security requirements throughout the employee and contractor lifecycle including screening, training, and offboarding.
Policy.title: HR Security Policy

Policy.policy_content:
Physical and Environmental Security Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: Facilities Manager  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes requirements for protecting the organisation's physical premises, server rooms, equipment, and environmental systems from unauthorised access, damage, or disruption.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all organisational premises, data centres, server rooms, and equipment including offices, remote working facilities, and cloud provider data centres (addressed via third-party attestations).

3. Physical Security Perimeters

Security perimeters shall be defined for all locations containing sensitive information or information processing facilities. Entry to these areas shall be controlled using electronic access controls (badge readers, PIN pads, or biometrics). Perimeters shall be clearly demarcated.

4. Access Controls

  • Access to server rooms and sensitive areas shall be restricted to authorised personnel only;
  • Visitor access shall be logged, escorted, and limited to areas required for the visit;
  • Access rights shall be reviewed quarterly and revoked promptly when no longer required;
  • Tailgating and piggybacking are strictly prohibited.

5. Physical Security Monitoring

  • CCTV shall be installed at all entry and exit points and in server rooms;
  • CCTV footage shall be retained for a minimum of 90 days;
  • Alarm systems shall be maintained in operational condition and tested regularly.

6. Equipment Security

  • All portable devices containing sensitive data must be encrypted;
  • Equipment shall not be removed from the premises without prior authorisation;
  • Unattended equipment must be locked or powered off;
  • Clean desk and clear screen policies shall be enforced.

7. Environmental Controls

Server rooms shall maintain appropriate environmental controls including:

  • Temperature and humidity monitoring with alerting;
  • Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) for critical systems;
  • Fire suppression systems;
  • Water leak detection.

8. Equipment Disposal

All equipment being decommissioned or disposed of shall have data securely wiped using NIST 800-88 compliant methods or physically destroyed. Disposal shall be documented and certificates of destruction obtained from third-party disposal providers.

9. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.7.1–A.7.13 and SOC 2 CC6.4.
Policy.description: Controls for protecting physical premises, equipment, and environmental systems from unauthorised access or damage.
Policy.title: Physical and Environmental Security Policy

Policy.description: Framework for identifying, assessing, treating, and monitoring information security risks across the organisation.
Policy.title: Risk Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Risk Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the organisation's framework for identifying, assessing, treating, and monitoring information security risks. It ensures that risks are managed systematically and in accordance with the organisation's risk appetite.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all information security risks across the organisation's systems, processes, people, and third-party relationships.

3. Risk Appetite

The organisation has a low risk appetite for information security risks that could result in:

  • Unauthorised disclosure of personal or customer data;
  • Loss of availability of critical systems beyond agreed RTO;
  • Regulatory non-compliance or enforcement action;
  • Reputational damage affecting customer trust.

4. Risk Assessment Methodology

Risks shall be assessed using a 5x5 likelihood-impact matrix. Scores are calculated as: Risk Score = Likelihood (1–5) × Impact (1–5).

ScoreRatingAction Required
20–25CriticalImmediate treatment required; escalate to Board
12–19HighTreatment plan required within 30 days; executive notification
6–11MediumTreatment plan required within 90 days
1–5LowMonitor; accept or treat as resources allow

5. Risk Treatment Options

  • Mitigate: Implement controls to reduce likelihood or impact;
  • Accept: Acknowledge and accept the risk within appetite (requires CISO and executive approval for medium+ risks);
  • Transfer: Transfer the risk via insurance, contracts, or outsourcing;
  • Avoid: Discontinue the activity that gives rise to the risk.

6. Risk Register

A formal risk register shall be maintained and reviewed quarterly by the CISO. The register shall document: risk description, owner, likelihood, impact, risk score, current controls, treatment option, treatment plan, target date, and residual risk score.

7. Risk Review

The full risk assessment shall be conducted at least annually and following significant changes including new systems, major incidents, or changes in the threat landscape. Risk treatment effectiveness shall be reviewed as part of the annual ISMS management review.

8. Related Controls

Supports SOC 2 CC3.1–CC3.4, CC9.1 and ISO 27001 Clause 6.1.

Policy.description: Defines requirements for the use of cryptographic controls and the management of cryptographic keys.
Policy.title: Cryptography and Key Management Policy
Policy.policy_content:
Cryptography and Key Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy defines the organisation's requirements for the use of cryptographic controls to protect information confidentiality, integrity, and authenticity. It establishes standards for cryptographic algorithms, key management, and the lifecycle of cryptographic materials.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all cryptographic implementations including data encryption, digital signatures, certificate management, and secure communications across all systems and applications.

3. Approved Cryptographic Standards

Use CaseApproved Algorithm / ProtocolMinimum Key Length
Symmetric encryption (data at rest)AES-GCM256-bit
Asymmetric encryption / key exchangeRSA, ECDHRSA: 2048-bit; ECDH: P-256 or higher
Digital signaturesRSA-PSS, ECDSARSA: 2048-bit; ECDSA: P-256
HashingSHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512N/A
Transport encryptionTLS 1.2, TLS 1.3N/A
Password hashingArgon2id, bcrypt, scryptWork factor per current OWASP guidance

Prohibited Algorithms

The following algorithms are prohibited due to known weaknesses: DES, 3DES, RC4, MD5, SHA-1, SSL 2.0/3.0, TLS 1.0/1.1.

4. Key Management

4.1 Key Generation

Cryptographic keys shall be generated using a cryptographically secure random number generator (CSPRNG). Key generation shall occur in a trusted, controlled environment.

4.2 Key Storage

Private keys and symmetric keys shall be stored securely using a Hardware Security Module (HSM) or an approved Key Management Service (KMS) such as AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Cloud KMS. Keys shall never be stored in plaintext on disk or in source code.

4.3 Key Rotation

  • TLS certificates: renewed before expiry (minimum 30 days prior);
  • Data encryption keys: rotated at least annually;
  • API keys and secrets: rotated at least every 90 days or upon suspected compromise.

4.4 Key Revocation and Destruction

Compromised or no longer required keys shall be revoked and securely destroyed immediately. Destruction shall be documented.

5. Certificate Management

A certificate inventory shall be maintained documenting all TLS/SSL certificates including expiry dates. Automated certificate monitoring and renewal shall be implemented where possible. Certificates shall only be issued by trusted Certificate Authorities (CAs).

6. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.8.24 and SOC 2 CC6.7.

Vulnerability Management Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CISO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes the organisation's requirements for identifying, prioritising, remediating, and monitoring technical vulnerabilities across all information systems and applications.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all systems within the organisation's information security boundary including servers, workstations, network devices, applications, cloud infrastructure, and mobile devices.

3. Vulnerability Scanning

  • Automated vulnerability scans shall be conducted at least weekly against all internet-facing systems and at least monthly against internal systems;
  • Application security testing (SAST, DAST) shall be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline;
  • An authenticated scan shall be used where technically feasible to detect patch status and configuration issues.

4. Patch and Remediation SLAs

CVSS SeverityRatingRemediation SLA
9.0–10.0Critical24 hours (or immediate compensating control)
7.0–8.9High7 calendar days
4.0–6.9Medium30 calendar days
0.1–3.9Low90 calendar days or risk accepted

5. Penetration Testing

External penetration testing shall be conducted by an accredited third-party firm at least annually covering external network, web applications, and where applicable, internal network and social engineering. Findings shall be remediated in accordance with the SLAs above. Test reports shall be retained for three (3) years.

6. Exception Management

Where a vulnerability cannot be remediated within the defined SLA (e.g., due to business impact of the patch), a formal exception shall be documented including: the vulnerability, reason for exception, compensating controls implemented, residual risk, and risk acceptance by the CISO. Exceptions shall be reviewed monthly.

7. Asset Coverage

An asset inventory shall be maintained to ensure complete vulnerability scan coverage. Newly deployed systems shall be included in scanning within 24 hours of deployment to production.

8. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.8.8 and SOC 2 CC7.1, CC7.2.
Policy.description: Establishes requirements for identifying, assessing, and remediating technical vulnerabilities in systems and applications.
Policy.title: Vulnerability Management Policy

Policy.policy_content:
Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC) Policy

Version: 1.0  |  Status: Draft  |  Owner: CTO  |  Review Date: May 2027

1. Purpose

This policy establishes requirements for integrating security into every phase of the organisation's software development lifecycle. It ensures that security is a foundational element of all software and systems developed, acquired, or maintained by the organisation.

2. Scope

This policy applies to all software development activities including internal development, custom application development, API development, and configuration of commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) software.

3. Security Requirements

Security requirements shall be defined at the outset of all development projects and include:

  • Authentication and authorisation requirements;
  • Data protection requirements based on data classification;
  • Input validation and output encoding requirements;
  • Logging and audit trail requirements;
  • Compliance requirements (privacy, regulatory).

4. Secure Design

Applications shall be designed following security-by-design principles:

  • Threat modelling shall be conducted for all new applications and significant changes;
  • Security architecture reviews shall be conducted before development commences;
  • OWASP Top 10 risks shall be explicitly addressed in application design.

5. Secure Coding Standards

All developers shall follow the organisation's secure coding guidelines which include:

  • Input validation and parameterised queries to prevent injection attacks;
  • Output encoding to prevent XSS;
  • Use of approved cryptographic libraries only;
  • Secure management of secrets (no hard-coded credentials);
  • Error handling that does not expose sensitive information.

6. Security Testing in CI/CD

  • SAST (Static Application Security Testing): Integrated into the CI pipeline; builds fail on critical or high findings;
  • SCA (Software Composition Analysis): Dependency vulnerability scanning on every build;
  • DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing): Run against staging environment before production deployment;
  • Secrets Scanning: Automated scanning to detect credentials committed to source code.

7. Code Review

All code changes must be reviewed by at least one other developer before merging to main/production branches. Security-sensitive changes (authentication, authorisation, cryptography) require review by a senior developer or the security team.

8. Environment Separation

Development, testing, staging, and production environments shall be strictly separated. Production data shall not be used in non-production environments. Access to production environments shall be restricted to authorised personnel.

9. Related Controls

Supports ISO 27001 A.8.25–A.8.29 and SOC 2 CC8.1.
Policy.description: Integrates security into the software development process from design through deployment and maintenance.
Policy.title: Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC) Policy

Task.description: Create detailed product/service description document, prepare data flow diagram showing CDR data handling, and provide step-by-step consumer journey walkthrough.

Task.description: Compile audited or draft financial statements from the last financial year with directors' declaration to demonstrate financial viability.

Task.description: Collect certificates of currency for all relevant policies (PI, cyber, PL), review insurance schedules, and obtain signed written insurance adequacy statement from authorized representative.
Task.title: Obtain and Review Insurance Policies and Adequacy Statement

Task.description: Document IDR process compliant with ASIC RG 271, establish complaint register template, and ensure complaint handling meets CDR Rules timeframes.
Task.title: Draft Internal Dispute Resolution (IDR) Process

Task.description: Confirm membership in relevant EDR scheme (e.g., AFCA), obtain membership certificate, document membership number and commencement date.
Task.title: Verify External Dispute Resolution (EDR) Scheme Membership

Task.description: Prepare completed assurance report covering CDR data environment in accordance with ACCC standards (ASAE 3150 or SOC 2 Type II) by qualified assurance practitioner.
Task.title: Prepare Information Security Assurance Report (ASAE 3150 or SOC 2 Type II)

Task.description: Review and implement CDR Privacy Safeguards (Schedule 2 CCA 2010) and Australian Privacy Principles. Document privacy handling procedures and obtain legal sign-off.
Task.title: Implement Privacy Safeguards and APPs Compliance

Task.description: Document data breach response procedures, establish breach notification processes within statutory timeframes, and prepare breach response playbook.
Task.title: Conduct Data Breach Response Capability Assessment

Task.description: Establish incident management procedures, define incident severity classifications, create escalation matrix, and prepare incident response playbook.
Task.title: Develop Incident Management and Cybersecurity Incident Response Plan

Task.description: Define RTO/RPO targets, establish backup and recovery procedures, conduct DR testing, and document resilience controls.
Task.title: Implement Operational Resilience and Business Continuity Controls

Task.description: Establish penetration testing schedule, implement vulnerability scanning procedures, define patch management SLAs, and maintain remediation log.
Task.title: Document Security Testing and Vulnerability Management Framework

Task.description: Deploy centralized logging for all CDR data access, establish audit log retention (minimum 7 years), define monitoring alerts, and test log integrity.

Task.description: Compile all required documentation, prepare accreditation application in ACCC format, conduct internal review, and submit application.

Task.description: Deploy phishing-resistant MFA (e.g. FIDO2/WebAuthn or authenticator app) for all staff accessing production systems, admin consoles, and cloud infrastructure. Document configuration and enrolment rates.
Task.title: Implement Multi-Factor Authentication on All Production Systems

Task.description: Implement a PAM tool to manage, monitor, and audit privileged account usage. Configure just-in-time access, session recording, and automated account lifecycle management.
Task.title: Deploy Privileged Access Management (PAM) Solution

Task.description: Perform a formal information security risk assessment across all in-scope systems, processes, and third parties. Document likelihood, impact, and risk scores. Produce the risk register and treatment plan.
Task.title: Conduct Enterprise-Wide Risk Assessment

Task.description: Prepare the management assertion letter in accordance with ASAE 3150 requirements, covering fair presentation of the system description, suitability of design, and operating effectiveness of controls. Obtain CEO and Board sign-off.

Task.description: Document the complete system description covering the five components: infrastructure, software, people, procedures, and data. Include service boundaries, subservice organisations, and CUECs.
Task.title: Write ASAE 3150 System Description Document

Task.description: Implement automated vulnerability scanning (internal and external), define patch SLAs (critical 24hr, high 7d, medium 30d), and establish exception and risk acceptance processes.
Task.title: Establish Vulnerability Management Programme

Task.description: Deploy a SIEM solution to centralise log collection, define correlation rules for key threat scenarios, and establish alert triage and escalation procedures.
Task.title: Implement Security Information and Event Management (SIEM)

Task.description: Engage an accredited third-party penetration testing firm to conduct external network, web application, and internal network penetration testing. Remediate findings within SLA.
Task.title: Conduct Penetration Test (External and Internal)

Task.description: Finalise all information security policies listed in the policies register. Obtain management approval and formally communicate to all staff. Record acknowledgements.
Task.title: Complete All Policy Documents and Obtain Approval

Task.description: Execute a full DR test including failover to secondary environment, data restore from backup, and RTO/RPO validation. Document results and remediate any failures.
Task.title: Conduct Disaster Recovery Test

Task.description: Deploy DLP tooling to detect and prevent unauthorised transmission of sensitive data via email, USB, cloud storage, and other channels. Define and tune DLP policies.
Task.title: Implement Data Loss Prevention (DLP) Controls

Task.description: Complete security assessments for all critical and high-risk third-party vendors. Review SOC 2 / ISO 27001 reports where available. Obtain contractual security commitments.
Task.title: Conduct Vendor Security Assessments for Critical Suppliers

Task.description: Deploy a security awareness training platform. Enrol all staff in mandatory annual training covering phishing, social engineering, data handling, and acceptable use. Run monthly phishing simulations.
Task.title: Establish Security Awareness Training Programme

Task.description: Segment production, development, and corporate networks using VLANs and firewall rules. Implement zero-trust network access for remote workers.
Task.title: Implement Network Segmentation

Task.description: Deploy EDR solution across all endpoints (servers, laptops, workstations). Configure detection rules and ensure 24/7 alert monitoring or SOC integration.
Task.title: Establish Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR)

Task.description: Conduct and document formal quarterly reviews of all user access rights across production systems. Remove or modify access that is no longer appropriate. Obtain manager sign-off.
Task.title: Perform Quarterly User Access Reviews

Task.description: Create the control-to-objective mapping matrix linking each control in the system description to the relevant control objective it addresses.
Task.title: Build ASAE 3150 Control-to-Objective Mapping Matrix

Task.description: Configure immutable (WORM) backups for all critical data. Test restore procedures monthly. Document RTO/RPO targets and validate against test results.
Task.title: Implement Immutable Backup Solution

Task.description: Document the incident response plan covering detection, containment, eradication, recovery, and communication. Conduct a tabletop exercise to validate the plan.
Task.title: Develop and Test Incident Response Plan

Task.description: Deploy a WAF in front of all public-facing web applications and APIs. Configure OWASP Top 10 rulesets and review and tune alerts monthly.
Task.title: Implement Web Application Firewall (WAF)

Task.description: Create a comprehensive asset register covering all hardware, software, cloud services, and data assets. Apply information classification labels per the classification policy.
Task.title: Build Asset Register and Classification Scheme

Task.description: Integrate security into the development pipeline including SAST, DAST, dependency scanning, and mandatory security review gates in CI/CD. Train developers on secure coding.
Task.title: Implement Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC) Controls

Task.description: Implement pre-employment background checking for all new hires including identity, employment history, and criminal record checks per the HR Security Policy.
Task.title: Conduct Staff Background Screening for New Hires

Task.description: Perform an internal pre-audit readiness assessment against all control objectives. Identify and remediate gaps before engaging the external assurance practitioner.
Task.title: Prepare ASAE 3150 Audit Readiness Assessment

Task.description: Ensure all databases, file stores, and backup media containing sensitive or personal information are encrypted at rest using AES-256 or equivalent. Document and verify configuration.
Task.title: Deploy Encryption for Data at Rest
